ANATOLIAN STUDIES
Building Phase I. the Basilica
(a) The marble pavement (Pavement A)
Rubbish and robber pits dug through the opus sectile pavement (here desig-
nated Pavement B) have revealed that there was an earlier floor beneath, here
designated Pavement A (Fig. 1). It is not possible to retrieve the complete plan
of this pavement, for traces are only visible in the later pits that have been dug
through both floors. Nevertheless, this damage enabled us to establish some fea-
tures of the earlier pavement's design and construction and provided the oppor-
tunity to examine parts of the foundations of the Phase I Basilica. The largest
portions of Pavement A that were exposed are found in the bema and, although
little could be seen at the western end of the naos, some traces were visible close
to the templon. It is clear from both pavements A and B that the bema had
always been higher than the naos pavement, the step up to the bema being the
stylobate of the templon. This factor, and the laying of Pavement B at a higher
level still in the bema, probably contributed to the better preservation of
Pavement A in this area. Elsewhere in the building Pavement B was not raised
as high, and so its construction seems to have destroyed most traces of
Pavement A.
A robber pit through the site of the altar revealed the foundation for
Pavement A, composed of limestone rubble, marble fragments and smaller
stones (Fig. 2). This miscellaneous material was closely packed together and had

a mortar cement poured over it (Context 57). A narrow gap through this layer
showed that soft earth lay beneath, presumably the pre-basilica ground surface.
On top of this raft of cemented rubble was laid a bed of pink opus signinum
mortar (Context 56). This mortar bed is of high quality, being hard, compact
and containing few air holes. Its pink colour derives from the large quantities of
crushed reddish-orange brick that was mixed with white lime. When exposed
108

AMORIUM EXCAVATIONS 1994
this mortar weathers to form the brownish surfaces already encountered in the
1993 season in the southern half of the bema and naos.
The opus signinum was smoothed to form a flat bedding, which extended
throughout the nave and bema. Phase I fittings still in situ, such as the western
portion of the templon stylobate and the altar base, are firmly set in this mortar
bed. The opus signinum thus formed a very solid and water-resistant foundation
for Pavement A and the architectural fittings. The cleaning of the synthronon
pavement also revealed packed and cemented rubble foundations covered by the
same opus signinum, which continues over the seats of the synthronon. The riser
and seat of the lowest tier are still largely covered, and isolated portions of opus
signinum remain on the upper surfaces of the structure. This evidence linking the
synthronon to Pavement A indicates that the synthronon was part of the primary
construction phase-a fact further suggested by its construction, which re-
sembles the upper portions of the Phase I walls.
In the bema, naos and synthronon a bed of white lime mortar (Context 55)
was found on top of the pink opus signinum. Context 55 is very hard and con-
tains few inclusions; it constituted the setting bed for the marble slabs of
Pavement A. Under these slabs this bed was spread thinly, being only c. 0.01 m.
thick. Where preserved, the ghost impressions of the robbed-out paving in the
white mortar bed indicate that in the bema Pavement A was composed of rec-
tangular slabs of marble, which were smooth on their undersides. The sloping
mortar ridges once between the slabs show that the sides of these slabs were bev-
elled on their edges. All these slabs are now missing, except for a few isolated
fragments of bluish-grey marble, mostly corners, that are still in situ (Context
54).
The synthronon was also paved in coloured marble at this time, for scraps
of marble floor tiles remain in the white mortar bed. A few panels of marble
revetment even remain in situ on the riser of the lowest seat, bedded in the opus
signinum. It may also be noted that, as on the synthronon, opus signinum mortar
was used to help fix the marble revetment to the walls of the basilica.4
As far as could be ascertained, the robbing of the marble from Pavement A
was fairly complete and probably systematic. It had certainly been removed
before the laying of the opus sectile Pavement B, for its under-bedding was laid
directly on top of the mortar bed of Pavement A. The same is true of the marble
wall revetment of the Phase I Basilica, which was almost entirely removed from
the surviving walls before they were plastered for wall paintings. The most likely
explanation is that, like the marble revetment, the marble paving slabs were
stripped to be cut up to form the floor and furnishings of the new building.5
(b) The templon stylobate and the solea
The templon stylobate (Context 37) is the most conspicuous fitting still in
situ, dividing the raised platform of the bema from the naos (Fig. 3). The stylo-
bate is carved from purple breccia with white veins, a Docimian marble, which
was used for other Late Antique fittings and furnishings at Amorium. Five
blocks, designated 1-5, form the western stylobate, running north to south
between walls 55 and 34 of the Phase II Church. The step on their western side
was finely carved with a cable and cavetto moulding that bears comparison with

the templon stylobate of the Basilica of St. John Studios at Constantinople.6
Like the Studios stylobate, none of the blocks are of the same length, some
being considerably shorter than their companions. The south and north sides of
the bema are also enclosed by a row of five stylobate blocks. The northern
blocks, 11-15, are buried beneath a rubble wall and so have only been partially
excavated, but the southern blocks, 6-10, have been cleared, although their ex-
terior profiles were buried and concealed by scraps of marble paving in the
Middle Byzantine Phase II.
Excavation at the foot of the western stylobate blocks 1-5 showed that they
were still firmly set in the opus signinum underbedding (Context 56) of Pavement
A. This evidence indicates that blocks 1-5 are still in situ from Phase I of the
Basilica, and that the building work of Phase II was carried out around them in
the Middle Byzantine period. This was probably because of the massive size and
weight of these stylobate blocks. It is worth noting that no attempt was made to
move the Phase I stylobate of the nave colonnades either, probably for the same
reason. The north and south ends of the west templon stylobate directly abut
Phase II walls. Return mouldings are not visible, but these may have been cut
off during the later reconstruction. Although the foundations of the north and
south stylobates were not examined this year, it would appear that either they
too are still in situ, or that they were moved slightly to avoid the Phase II piers
without disturbing the order of their blocks. Thus in the Phase I chancel
arrangement it is clear that, as in the basilica of St. John Studios at
Constantinople, the Amorium templon formed a n-shaped enclosure around the
altar, which projected c. 6.00 m. into the naos from the line of the apse and the
edge of the synthronon pavement.
Heavily worn surfaces on the front edges of blocks 2-3 and 7-8 indicate
entrances at the centres of the western and southern sides of the templon. Each
of these thresholds are framed by rectangular cuttings for baluster posts and
have wear marks caused by the movement of doors. The northern side is still
largely buried, but there is a socket in block 12 directly opposite the southern
threshold, indicating the presence of a third doorway on the north side. This
arrangement also closely parallels that of St. John Studios at Constantinople.7
Apart from the sockets either side of these entrances, there are no other sockets
in the stylobate surface. The surface is roughened, however, and bears traces of
pink opus signinum mortar, indicating that the stone superstructure must simply
have been cemented onto the stylobate. These markings are further complicated
by later markings from the Phase II templon, but further study may enable a
reconstruction of the layout of the Phase I screen.
As in other Late Antique basilicas, a solea extended westwards from the
central or Holy Doors (Tot
c,ytca 0upia) of the templon (Fig. 3). Two rectangular
blocks of bluish-grey breccia (designated 16-17), which had been reused in the
Phase II solea, can be identified as part of this structure. The blocks had been
prised from their original settings during the post-Byzantine period but clearly
had originally been set in the opus signinum underbedding of Phase I. Both
blocks have two rectangular sockets, like the ones on the templon stylobate, for
fitting baluster posts. Block 16 also had a round socket, still containing metal,

6.00 m. by 8.10 m. that is enclosed by the templon stylobate and the synthronon

most damaged part of the bema pavement, for a large pit (Context 47) has been
dug directly through its centre. This activity destroyed the greater part of the

The opus signinum mortar bed (Context 56) supported the altar base, as it
did the Phase I floor, Pavement A, but under the altar it was further strength-
ened by the laying of a course of rectangular and square bricks, measuring
0-20 m. by c. 0.37 m. and 0-34 sq. m. respectively. Some of these bricks had been
cut through and removed by the later pit, leaving only their impressions, but
their arrangement was clear. Bricks possess good tectonic properties and so
would have helped to support the heavy weight of the marble base and altar
table above. The same structural underpinning of opus signinum and brick can
be found under the altar base of the Basilica of St. Mary at Ephesus, built in
the early fifth century.8
The altar base at Amorium is badly damaged but its ghost impression in
the mortar and four fragments remain in situ, thus enabling its reconstruction
and the identification of detached pieces (for example, T576-577). These remains
show that the base was a large rectangular slab of white marble (Context 61),
measuring c. 2.04 m. north-south by 1 04 m. east-west. This base was set flat in
the opus signinumn bed (Context 56) to a depth of 0.02 m. The slab has a plain
border and moulded inner frame, the edge of which formed a shallow step raised
above the surrounding pavement. The moulded frame enclosed a flat sunken
panel which supported the altar table itself (P1. XIII (a)).9
The top surfaces of the base are highly polished, but one corner fragment
still in situ and the two loose pieces so far identified have roughened patches,
measuring c. 0-05 m. square, that indicate where the legs of the free-standing
altar table (il acyiac Tp&ticta) once stood. The heavy altar table, which was prob-
ably of marble, must have been supported by at least four, and more probably
by six or even nine marble legs.'? No fragments of this altar table have been
identified so far.
Although the centre of the altar base is destroyed, it is possible that it once
contained a consecration deposit or enkainion in the form of a small reliquary
casket or larnakidion, similar to those placed beneath other altar tables of the
fifth and sixth centuries." A fragment of a reliquary casket carved of imported
Thessalian verde antique and stylistically datable to the fifth-sixth centuries was,
in fact, excavated in the Lower City Church in 1991.12

Building Phase II. The Cross-Domed Basilica Church
(a) The opus sectile pavement (Pavement B)
In addition to the c. 80-00 sq. m. uncovered in 1993, excavation this year
has revealed up to 70-00 sq. m. of the opus sectile pavement, here designated
Pavement B. It now seems likely that the pavement covers the entire bema and
naos within the Phase I colonnade stylobates, an area of some 220-00 sq. m. The
remainder of the floor will be excavated in 1995. At present it is unknown
whether opus sectile decoration extends into the central sections of the north
and south aisles, but it is certain from excavation that the pavement here was
approximately 0.20 m. higher than in the naos. The western and eastern bays of
the north aisle were paved with terracotta floor tiles, but there is evidence for
some marble paving at the west end of the south aisle.3 When completely
uncovered, the Amorium pavement will be one of the largest excavated in any
Byzantine church (P1. XIII (b)).
It is now clear that, following the removal of almost all the marble paving
slabs of the Phase I Pavement A in the bema and the naos, the level of these
floors were raised substantially (Fig. 5). Sections created by later pits and stone-

earth mixed with crushed mortar, sometimes packed with large quantities of
loose marble chippings (Context 52). These chippings were clean, sharp scales of
bluish-grey marble, varying in size from 0-05 m. to 0-001 m. or less. Spreads of
white marble chips of the same kind and crushed brick were also found in this

earth in the bema and immediately in front of the central west door to the naos.
The condition of these marble chippings strongly suggests that they are waste
from stone carving, some of which may have resulted from cutting up slabs for
the pavement. The nature of the flakes also suggests, however, that they result
from major sculptural work. It is perhaps of some significance that many of the
Phase I stones heavily reworked in the Middle Byzantine period are of bluish-
grey marble.14 This packing between the floors clearly varied throughout the
building. Immediately west of the templon on the north side of the naos a mix-
ture of black and reddish-orange earth was closely packed with broken brick
and tile (Context 59).
Above this packing layer was a bed of hard white mortar, coarse and lumpy
in texture (Context 53 east of the templon, Context 58 west of the templon). The
thicker, larger paving slabs were set in this mortar bed, and fragments of marble
slabs, stones and the occasional brick fragment were set in the mortar ridges to
give additional support to the slabs. This diverse material was probably dis-
carded waste, and some fragments from the Phase I Basilica were identifiable. In
the south bema a fragment of dentilled beading from the Phase I wall revetment
is still embedded in the mortar, as is a fragment of an incised marble floor tile.
The small marble tiles (Context 36 east of the templon, Context 39 west of
the templon) were set in hard but brittle pink mortars of fine lime, mixed with
small grade gravel and crushed brick (Context 38 east of the templon, 40 west of
the templon). These mortars and tiles were laid on top of Contexts 53/58 and
surrounded the large marble slabs. This evidence shows that, when the pavement
was being laid, the large marble slabs were set in Contexts 53/58 first, creating a
framework for the artisans. Contexts 38/40 and the marble tiles were then in-
serted around this grid. Finally, the interstices between the marble tiles were
grouted with a very fine opus signinum mortar that rendered the pavement
waterproof. Most of these slabs and tiles had been cut from the marble revet-
ment, pavement and other furniture fragments that had been stripped from the
Phase I Basilica.15
Although many marble tiles had been robbed out and the pavement had
been cut by fourteen later pits in the northern half of the bema alone, the design
of the pavement is clear (Fig. 1). This can be reconstructed from the surviving
areas of the floor itself and from the ghost impressions of the marble slabs and
tiles in the mortar bed. The bema continued to be demarcated by the massive
stylobate of the Phase I templon, but on its north and south sides its blocks were
used only as framing slabs for the pavement rather than as a step.
To the west of the altar the pavement was paved with square marble slabs
divided by borders composed of square marble tiles with cut off corners ranged
around diamonds. East of the altar the pavement comprised rectangular panels
bordered by octagonal tiles. A band to the north of these panels and the altar
had a more elaborate design of rectanglar tiles framed by diamonds and tri-
angles. A line of large rectangular and square slabs divided the central area of
the bema from its northern sector, the latter being paved with three rectangular
panels surrounded respectively by frames of lozenges, diamonds and octagons,
within a framework of plain marble slabs.
West of the templon the pavement parallel to the stylobate is paved with
large terracotta bricks or floor tiles similar to those in the equivalent location on

of the pavement in the Middle Byzantine period or are part of the original
design. As found on the south side in 1993, a band of bisected diamonds edged
by triangles ran in front of the templon stylobate. West of this once lay a large
rectangular slab, later destroyed by a pit, framed by square tiles with cut-off
corners arranged around diamonds. The corners of this frame were made up of
irregularly-shaped lozenges. Immediately to the south and north of this panel
was a line of plain rectangular slabs, while the north corner beside Wall 55 was
paved with an irregular mixture of materials.
The pavement in Trench Al in the western portion of the naos was richly
designed but not as intricate as that in the bema (Fig. 6, P1. XIV (a)). The cen-
tral strip of the pavement was almost as wide as the western threshold and was
the most elaborate section. This zone was decorated with large rectanglar slabs
surrounded by frames of square- and lozenge-shaped tiles and circular panels of
marble with circular frames. That nearest to the ambo contained the glass floor
mosaic excavated in 1993.17 This central strip is oriented on the same axis as the
west door, the ambo, the solea, the templon door and the altar, thus forming a
visual and possibly processional route through the building. To either side of
this central strip the pavement was also divided into rectangular panels with
inlaid frames, the northern side being the better preserved. Unfortunately, the
presence of the late rubble wall on the north side of the naos prevented the
excavation of the area between the piers immediately in front of the north
colonnade stylobate, but the pavement in the corresponding area to the south
was decorated with large marble discs, surrounded by a plain frame and
octagonal tiles.
(b) The Templon and the Solea
In Phase II only blocks 1-5 of the western part of the Phase I stylobate
seem to have supported a templon. The cuttings and wear marks on the stylo-
bate show that the single entrance through the templon on the western side was
retained (Fig. 3). There is no evidence for another entrance, which in this phase
was closed off at its north and south ends by Walls 34 and 55. There are rough-
ened areas on the top surface of the stylobate which seem to indicate the pres-
ence of pillars or columns and intercolumniations, but the actual design of the
templon and how it relates to the pieces so far excavated in the Church still
needs further consideration. It is clear, however, that the stone closure slabs
were fitted into Walls 34 and 55 at the ends of the templon, for a deep socket
had been carved out of the face of each pier for the insertion of a stone slab
capped with a heavy rail (P1. XIV (b)). Unfortunately, the walls are not pre-
served high enough to indicate where the heavy stone epistyle of the templon was
inserted.
An interesting find was a stump of a narrow wall resting on block 1 of the
templon stylobate and attached to Wall 55. This wall was built of rough
boulders and other stones, crudely held together with a coarse white plaster con-
taining large amounts of crushed gypsum. The wall was so poorly built that
large stones protruded from its exterior faces, which had been smoothed to form
uneven wall surfaces. No paint was found on the exteriors of this wall, and it
was certainly of later construction than the slab socket in Wall 55, for it

As in Phase I, the central Holy Door in the templon was approached via a
solea that connected the bema to the ambo. Only the stylobate of this solea sur-
vives in situ. It utilised the remaining northern stylobate of the Phase I solea,
which was enlarged by placing next to it a matching bluish-grey marble door-
jamb (partially excavated in 1993) that still has its mouldings intact (Fig. 3). The
door-jamb was cemented on top of the Phase II pavement and its mouldings
were hidden by a tile and marble skirting. The central surface of the solea stylo-
bate was well worn, indicating its use as a walkway. The southern side of the
jamb still had traces of mortar, cuttings and sockets containing fragments of
lead and iron dowels located opposite the sockets of the Phase I solea. These
traces probably indicate the locations of baluster posts of the Phase II
parapet.
Excavation on the south side of the bema showed that the templon stylobate
was buried in Phase II and was used as a part of the paving when the floor level
was raised. A new south entrance to the bema was created between two new
piers, Walls 32 and 34 (Fig. 3). Between these piers a raised step was created
using two large spolia. The piece to the west was a Late Roman (?) cornice
block, carved from limestone with a cable and cavetto moulding; the eastern
piece was an uncarved limestone block. Four square sockets for piers or baluster
posts are cut in the top surface of the step, and the eastern and western inter-
columniations are roughened for siting closure slabs. The central intercolumnia-
tion is wider than the other two and is well worn on its edge, indicating an
entrance. Traces of wear from the movement of a gate or door are also visible
beside the eastern socket. At the centre of this entrance on its northern side is
another socket for a bolt, and the step is worn in an arc to either side, indicating
that a two leaved door led into the bema area. The arch between the piers must,
therefore, have been closed off by a screen, most probably of stone, with a single
central door, which led from the south-eastern bay of the south aisle into the
bema. This door was not aligned with the entrance marked on the Phase I
templon stylobate, which in any case had been dismantled by this time.
(c) The Altar Site
The Phase I altar base was completely buried beneath the Phase II
Pavement B; mortar still overlies the edges of the marble foundation and
adheres to its upper surface (Fig. 4, P1. XIII (a)). Its Middle Byzantine successor
must have been built on the same site, for the opus sectile pavement shows that
there is no placement for an altar anywhere else within the bema. This altar has
been completely destroyed by the large pit (Context 47), but it seems to have
been smaller in extent than its predecessor, for marble tiles and mortar beds still
in situ run over the edges of the Phase I marble base. Two large fragments of a
limestone block carved with four table leg sockets found loose in the Church
may be the foundation of the Middle Byzantine altar table. As yet no evidence
has been found for a ciborion, but this may also have been destroyed by later
occupation and disturbance.
(d) Wall Paintings
Campaigns in the Church since 1990 have brought to light large quantities
of painted wall plaster, and substantial sections are still preserved in situ on the
118

AMORIUM EXCAVATIONS 1994
interior walls.18 Considerable amounts were also found during this year's exca-
vation, particularly where they had fallen at the foot of Wall 55 in the bema. A
number of loose stone blocks that had fallen from collapsed upper walls but still
retained their frescoes were also conserved and inventoried. Most of these frag-
ments illustrated patches of colour, but one preserved recognisable iconography.
A single layer of painted plaster was preserved. This is painted with a dark blue
ground and part of a nimbus in golden yellow paint, within which there is a rib-
bon from a crown. The fillet is twisted and projects to the side of the subject's
head, suggesting that this fragment once formed part of the figure of an angel.
Thus, although the study of the painted plaster is in its early stages, it is already
clear that the walls of the church were decorated with figural frescoes.19
Post-Byzantine Occupation
The layers dug this year in the northern half of the bema (Trench A3-1)
overlying the floor proved to be particularly clear and undisturbed. They have
greatly helped to illuminate the stratigraphy of the southern half of the bema
and naos, excavated and recorded by Paul Cox in 1993. The results from these
two years' work can now be collated and provide sufficient information for the
interpretation of the post-Byzantine and Turkish use of the Church. These con-
clusions will be published separately elsewhere.
Conclusion
Excavation in 1994 has greatly clarified the relative chronology of the
Lower City Church and has demonstrated the important contribution the
excavation and study of this structure is making to our knowledge of Byzantine
ecclesiastical architecture in Anatolia. It has shown that the Phase I Basilica was
well built of high-quality materials that find parallels at other metropolitan
centres. It has also revealed the nature of the liturgical furnishings in the bema
and so will assist in the reconstruction of their use. Further excavation and
study of the building will hopefully provide a more precise foundation date for
the Phase I Basilica, but a date sometime in the late fifth century, based upon
evidence of the marble capitals, seems probable.20
The scale of the Middle Byzantine remodelling of the Church and the evi-
dence that some furnishings were not or could not be reused, together with the
thoroughness of the removal of the marble from the walls and floors, gives some
idea of the magnitude of the catastrophe that overtook the old basilica and
rendered it unusable. The building that replaced it, which may be recognised as
a Cross-domed Basilica Church, was conceived on a grand scale for a provincial
location. Its dynamic architecture was embellished with one of the grandest opus
sectile pavements yet excavated, together with wall paintings on its lower walls
and polychrome glass mosaics adorning the vaults. In addition, the fittings and
furnishings found in the second church indicate a major sculptural scheme. So
far over 500 fragments of Byzantine sculpture from the Church have been
inventoried, making the Amorium collection amongst the largest excavated in
Turkey. Gold-glass mosaic was the most expensive form of Byzantine mural
decoration, and the fragments recovered so far certainly attest to the quality
and scale of decoration in the Phase II bema. Although they are fragmentary,

these mosaics are another indication of the lavishness of the Phase II Church
and of the wealth and stature of its, as yet, unknown patron(s). Work upon the
exact dating of the reconstruction, based on archaeological, architectural and
artistic evidence, is now underway, but the painted plaster may provide an
important terminus post quem. Since the earliest painted decoration in the Phase
II Church was figural, this indicates that the decoration, and probable recon-
struction of the building, took place after the final defeat of Iconoclasm in 843.
Sculptural and painted plaster fragments also suggest that the building was later
refurbished, perhaps in the middle decades of the eleventh century.
A terminus post quem for the post-Byzantine use of the Church is suggested
by the ending of its sacred use, probably some time after the Seljuk conquest of
the area in the 1070s. How much time elapsed between its abandonment as a
church and the robbing of its marbles is not yet known, nor is it certain when
the building was reoccupied and put to secular uses by its new owners. Nor has
any evidence been discovered so far to suggest that it was transformed into a
mosque. It is certain, however, that large parts of the structure stood intact and
were reused, perhaps for several centuries.
THE LOWER CITY, TRENCH AB (Deniz Atilla & Tugba Tanyeri)
The season's work on the Lower City fortifications focussed on the gateway
to the west of the triangular tower. The masonry debris was planned and then
partially removed, while the trench was extended north-eastwards into the city,
the aim being to come down onto a road surface through the gate and to follow
this into the city. It was found that the gateway had been blocked by a crude
stone wall built across its northern, inner end. This wall contained reused
blocks, including a keystone decorated with a raised cross medallion
(PI. XV (a)). Other rubble walls found to the rear of the gateway may be associ-
ated with the Middle Byzantine reoccupation of the Lower City fortifications
and probably relate to the construction of dwellings in this area in the
tenth-eleventh centuries, for which good evidence has been found in previous
seasons.21 Few finds were recovered from the rubble collapse inside the gateway,
but the trench extending into the city produced more material, including disar-
ticulated human remains, one small fragment of a Greek inscription and two
examples of the "Vandal" type of terrracotta lamp.22 Apart from a number of
late rubble walls, the sector within the city was largely devoid of features. One
wall, however, clearly belongs to a more substantial structure, and this will be
investigated during the coming season.
THE UPPER CITY, TRENCH ST (Kate Hughes)
This season's work has greatly clarified the chronology and stratigraphy of
the various features that were first uncovered last year, and eight distinct phases
have now been identified. It was decided to leave Step 3 at the level exposed last
year. The archaeology of this step appears to be considerably earlier than that of
Steps 1 and 2, and without removing the masonry in these two steps it is not
possible to establish the relationship between the earlier and the later strati-
graphy.
A wall (Context 123), constructed from a course of very large, roughly rec-
tangular blocks of limestone, lying horizontally above foundation courses of
21AS XLI (1991), 221-2 and fig. 4; AS XLIII (1993), 151 and pl. XXVII (b).
22Compare AS XLII (1992), 219 and fig. 6.11-12.
120

AMORIUM EXCAVATIONS 1994
irregularly-shaped blocks, was exposed across the width of the trench at the
north edge of Step 2. In its method of construction it has a direct analogy in
Trench AB, where the main fortification wall has just such a course of hori-
zontal blocks, with faced, vertically-positioned blocks directly above them, and
rubble foundations below. So, this wall may tentatively be interpreted as the
remains of an early defensive wall to the Upper City.
Another substantial east-west orientated wall (Context 128), part of which
was exposed last year in the east side of the trench, revealing that it contained
reused masonry blocks, was shown to have been largely robbed out at a later
date but prior to the construction of the surviving circuit wall that marks the
south end of the trench (P1. XV (b)). It would seem that when this last fortifica-
tion wall was built the side of the Upper City was extensively remodelled in
order to create a steep slope or glace as an additional defensive feature in front
of the wall. There is also a limited amount of evidence to suggest that there was
once a paved surface immediately in front of the city wall, a feature that may
find a parallel in the surface excavated in 1993 in Trench L in front of the main
wall.23
THE UPPER CITY, TRENCH TT (Simon Mortimer & Mercan Araydin)
A new 30-metre trench (PI. XVI (a)) was opened on the north side of the
Upper City, immediately to the south of the Step Trench and within the circuit
wall whose outer face was uncovered last year. Initial aims in excavating Trench
TT were to investigate the interior of the Upper City and to gain further insight
into the stratigraphy of the mound. Excavation exposed a range of late Seljuk
and early Ottoman features and surfaces, including a well, an oven, possible
courtyard surfaces, and a room with fallen roof timbers still preserved in situ.
The extensive nature of these associated deposits and the fact that they are gen-
erally better preserved than those encountered in previous seasons on the south
side of the Upper City (in Trench L) allowed us to gain a better insight into the
final stages of the permanent occupation of Amorium in the fourteenth-early
fifteenth century.
During the removal of one of the many of the smaller walls and rubble
scatters associated with this late medieval occupation, a skull was found incor-
porated into a sub-floor surface. Further excavation across the trench, especially
beneath the room floor surface unearthed several other disarticulated human
bones. These remains probably represent a large number of scattered burials,
and it suggests very strongly firstly that there was a cemetery in the vicinity of
the present trench, and secondly that this was a Christian (i.e. Byzantine) ceme-
tery, desecrated by the later inhabitants of the Upper City.
POTTERY
(Claudia Wagner)
Approximately 8,000 sherds of pottery were recovered from the four areas
excavated this season. The quantity of material proved too substantial for all of
it to be studied in detail during the season. Nevertheless, all of the sherds were
classified by their fabric, but only two-thirds of the diagnostic sherds, rims and
bases, have been matched with the typology of shapes established over the past
five years by Lucy Bown. The fabric type series was augmented by eight new
coarse ware and four fine ware fabrics, the shape typology gained 120 new
coarse ware and 62 fine ware forms. Amongst the latter one may note especially
23See AS XLIV (1994), 113 and pi. XIX (b).
121

ANATOLIAN STUDIES
the presence of two fragments of "Sagalassos" ware, indicating that Sagalassos
not only served to provide pottery to local markets in Pisidia but also exported
north to Central Anatolia as well as south to Pamphylia and the Mediterranean
coast.24
Excavations in the Church produced very little pottery and, as in previous
years, the material seems to be mainly Seljuk, mixed with residual Byzantine,
Late Roman and other earlier pottery. Most of the Seljuk potsherds consisted of
cooking wares, together with a number of glazed sgraffito vessels. Trench AB,
the area of the Gateway, also yielded very little pottery. The sherds consist
mostly of the normal coarse ware fabrics, mainly Fabric 1 and Fabric 2; dating
of this material will have to await a more detailed study of their shapes.
The largest quantities of pottery were recovered from the two trenches in
the Upper City (ST and TT). The former, in particular, contained in a re-
deposited context a relatively high percentage of late Roman fine wares, while
the bulk of the material from the latter consists predominately of coarse wares,
usually associated with simple domestic use. Cooking pots and jars, together
with jugs, are the types most commonly found. Trench TT, however, also pro-
duced a fragment of a Miletus-ware dish (Fabric 94), made at iznik in the late
fifteenth century.
SKELETAL
REMAINS (Kate Hughes)
At the beginning of the 1993 season two simple graves were uncovered in
the eastern sector of Trench L on the Upper City mound. Neither contained any
grave goods or traces of a coffin; both may be regarded as burials belonging to
the last half millennium.
The first burial (Context 340) contained an intact skeleton lying supine with
its right leg semi-flexed. The right arm was extended by the side, and the left
arm was tucked under the hip, while the head was turned to the left. The body
was orientated east-west, having the head pointing towards the west. The strati-
graphic position of the burial is uncertain, but it appears to post-date the adja-
cent structural features. The majority of sexually diamorphic features of the
skull and pelvis suggests that the skeleton is female. This conclusion is support-
ed by the gracile nature of the bones. On the other hand, metric measurements
(for example, the vertical diameter of the head of the humerus and the femur)
indicate large bone articulations that are normally indicative of a male. These
characteristics may, however, be explained by demographic variations, and on
balance it would seem more likely that the deceased was female. All the heads
of the long bones are fused, although the fusion line on the head of the femur is
still visible, suggesting that this had only recently occurred. The medial clavicle,
however, is not yet fused, while the sacral vertebrae are only partially fused.
This evidence indicates that death occurred in early adulthood, while study of
the sternal ends of the ribs also placed the skeleton in the 25-30 age group. The
bones of the right arm are in each case approximately 5 mm. longer than the
equivalent bones in the left arm, so it can be assumed that the deceased was
right-handed. Finally, the size of the skeletal remains would indicate that the
person was about 1-64 m. tall.
The skeleton had some interesting anomalies. On each scapula the epiphysis
of the acromion is unfused, which is untypical of an individual aged 25-30. Such
24S. Mitchell, E. Owens and M. Waelkens, "Ariassos and Sagalassos 1988", AS XXXIX
(1989), 174.
122

AMORIUM EXCAVATIONS 1994
fusion is usually assigned to an earlier age group between 15 and 23 years old.25
It may be a genetic anomaly or, alternatively, it may be related to the occupa-
tion of the deceased. Likewise, the fact that the conoid tubercle of the left
clavicle is greatly enlarged in comparison with its right counterpart may be
explained as occupation-related. The most remarkable feature of the skeleton,
however, was the very poor state of the teeth. Three teeth in the maxilla or
upper jaw (both right premolars and the right third molar) had been destroyed
by caries. The decayed premolars are also associated with periapical abscesses. It
is clear that the individual was also suffering from severe periodontal disease,
whereby the tooth roots had been exposed as a result of supragingival calculus
and alveolar resorption. The mandibular third molars are congenitally absent,
while the maxillary incissors are prognathic (the front teeth protruded noticeably
in front of the lower jaw), so that even when not contorted by acute toothache
the person's face would have been disfigured by unsightly buck teeth. The
advanced state of dental decay in a relatively young individual suggests a diet
rich in carbohydrates and may be taken as indicative of the fairly recent date of
deposition.
The second burial (Context 351) had been disturbed in the past and as a
result most of the left side of the skeleton was missing; the skull was also absent.
In addition, the lower legs and feet lay beyond the edge of the trench and so
were not recovered. The skeleton was lying supine with the arms to the sides and
has the same orientation as the first. The position of the right femur suggests
that the right leg was semi-flexed. In the west-facing section of Trench L, the
grave appears to be cut from the level of the subsoil, suggesting that the burial
post-dates the surrounding architecture. Despite the partial nature of the skele-
ton's survival, the right pelvis exhibits sexually diamorphic features that show
clearly that it is male. This conclusion is confirmed by the metrical measure-
ments of the long bones. In addition, the robusticity of the limbs and their pro-
nounced muscular attachments suggest that the individual was heavily built. It is
more difficult to be certain about the age of the deceased because so much of
the skeleton is missing. Nevertheless, inspection of the sternal ends of the ribs,
combined with evidence from the articular surfaces of the remaining vertebrae,
indicates that an age of 50+ may be assigned for this skeleton. Again, the height
of this individual may be estimated as about 1.73 m.
The pathology of this skeleton is noteworthy, for it displays signs of severe
degenerative arthritis of the right elbow joint. As this is unilateral (the left elbow
is not affected), this implies that it is a secondary condition which developed as
a consequence of an abnormality in the joint capsule, possibly caused by trauma
or an inflamation of the joint. The arthritis affected the distal humerus, the
proximal ulna and the radius and, as a result of the bone changes, it appears
that the individual could not fully extend his right arm and always had a par-
tially flexed elbow. Degenerative arthritic changes were also noted in the second
metacarpal and second proximal phalange of the right hand. Finally, on the
posterior shaft of the right femur the gluteal tuberosity, pectineal line and the
linea aspera are roughened and enlarged to form a distinct bony spur. Such pro-
nounced muscle attachments on the back of the femur have been linked to fre-
quent horse-riding activity.
The condition of the bones differed from one skeleton to the other, those of
the first being much better preserved than those of the second. The latter were
25T. D. White, Human Osteology, San Diego 1991, 313 and fig. 16.3.
123

ANATOLIAN STUDIES
extremely spongy, and the vertebrae and pelvis had started to disintegrate. Both
skeletons were therefore treated chemically to consolidate them.26 They remain
in storage on site.
Although the precise date of these two burials cannot be ascertained, some
conclusions may be drawn. Firstly, it would seem from their similar orientation
and deposition that they were both buried by the same cultural group. Secondly,
although the cut for the second burial (Context 351) is associated with the sub-
soil stratum, this does not necessarily mean that the burials took place within
(say) the last century. Indeed, this is unlikely for a number of reasons. The mod-
ern village, founded in 1892, established its cemetery outside the perimeters of
the former settlement site on the sloping hillside across the stream that runs
along the west side of the Upper City mound. Time must also be allowed for the
subsequent disturbance of the second grave, and results from 1993 indicated that
at least on the north side of the Upper City the subsoil contained material that
could be dated to the second half of the sixteenth century.27 It is, therefore,
tempting to associate these burials with the evidence from elsewhere in Trench L
which suggested that from the early Ottoman period the mound of Amorium
was sporadically used by Turkoman bands for their camp-site.
ENVIRONMENTAL
RESEARCH (John Giorgi)
Environmental sampling, initiated in 1992, continued with the collection of
further soil samples during the excavations in 1993 and 1994.28 The intention
was to recover carbonised plant remains and animal bone, which was also hand-
collected during the digging, in order to obtain more information on the range
of plants and animals exploited over time by the inhabitants of Amorium, on
crop and animal husbandry practices and on agricultural trade in the area.
There follow some preliminary comments on the nature of the carbonised
plant assemblages recovered from a number of these samples, although it is
possible that initial observations may be revised with the processing of the
remaining samples and further sorting of the flots. The animal bone has been
washed, dried and stored but has yet to be analysed.
Sampling and retrieval methods
A total of 46 soil samples was collected from a range of features in
Trenches L (3 samples), AB (6 samples), TT (18 samples) and the Church
(8 samples). The average size of the samples was 10 litres.
In addition, a sondage (RD) was excavated through possible rubbish
deposits exposed in the bank of a seasonal stream to the east of the Lower City
walls. A vertical section was recorded and eleven samples of 10 litres were col-
lected in 20 cm. spits for the recovery of carbonised plant remains, respecting
obvious stratigraphical boundaries. Bulk samples of up to 30 litres were also
taken adjacent to the same spits and dry-sieved through a garden-sized mesh for
26Initial conservation was carried out by Karen Barker during excavation in 1993, and it is
largely to her credit that the skeletons were still in such a good state of preservation when this
study was undertaken. Further cleaning was carried out by Kate Hughes before detailed study was
undertaken. Any bones that were beginning to disintegrate were consolidated with a PVA-water
(1:3) solution, while those that were already broken were reconstructed with undiluted PVA
27See AS XLIV (1994), 127.
28See AS XLIII (1993), 151-3.
124

AMORIUM EXCAVATIONS 1994
the retrieval of environmental data, particularly animal bone and artefactual
fragments, which were useful for both their intrinsic value and dating purposes.
Thirty-four of these samples were completely or partially processed, using a
simple method of bucket flotation onto a 0.25 mm. mesh, while the residues
were wet-sieved to 1 mm. The residues were dried and sorted for the recovery of
environmental and artefactual data, while the flots were dried and sorted using a
binocular microscope.
Preliminary results
Lower City, Trench AB. The six samples from the 1993 excavations within the
Triangular Tower produced very rich charred assemblages associated with the
violent destruction and burning of the tower. The sample from Context 330 pro-
duced small quantities of cereal grains, a large amount of chaff and weed seeds,
and frequent charcoal fragments, which appear to represent the residues, partic-
ularly the by-products, from crop-processing activities. Cereal grains included
six-row hulled barley (Hordeum vulgare), wheat (Triticum sp.) and millet
(Panicum miliaceum), together with barley and wheat rachis fragments, including
fragments belonging to hexaploid free-threshing bread wheat (Triticum aestivum
s.l.), plus culm nodes and internodes. A number of potential arable weeds were
also present, including corn gromwell (Polygonum aviculare agg.) and a number
of grasses-for example, darnel/brome (LoliumlBromus spp.). Occasional seeds
of charred grape (Vitis vinifera) and mineralised Rubus seeds were also
recovered.
Small samples (up to 500 ml) from Contexts 316, 318, 322, 327 and 328
produced very similar and rich assemblages dominated by large quantities of
straw, nodes and internodes and other plant stem fragments. Other plant parts
included florets and a range of seeds, particularly those belonging to plants
characteristic of arable grassland (for example, LoliumlBromus spp.,
MedicagolTrifolium
spp.) and wetland environments (for example, buttercups-
Ranunculus spp., sedges-Carex spp., and spike-rush-Eleocharis spp.).
Occasional grains of barley were also recovered.
These assemblages probably represent the residues of hay/straw harvested
for animal fodder and bedding or, possibly, for building (thatching/flooring)
purposes. The presence of sedges and other wetland plants suggest that some of
this material was collected from meadow/marshland areas.29 Further sorting and
analysis of these rich samples should provide more detailed information on the
character and origin of the assemblages.
The Lower City Church: Seven of the eleven samples from the Church produced
similar charred plant assemblages, consisting virtually entirely of the mixed
residues, particularly the by-products (chaff, weeds), from a number of crop-
processing activities. Cereal, represented by a small numbers of grains and large
quantities of chaff, included six-row barley, hexaploid bread wheat, wheat, mil-
let and, possibly, rye (cf. Secale cereale). A range of arable weed seeds was rep-
resented in the samples, with particularly large numbers of seeds belonging to
bedstraw (Galium spp.) and darnel/brome (LoliumlBromus spp.). Legume seeds
were present in moderate numbers, although these have yet to be separated
between possible cultivated and wild taxa. The samples also contained large
29For the local geography, see AS XXXVIII (1988), 175 and fig. 1.
125

ANATOLIAN STUDIES
numbers of seeds of Medicago/Trifolium
spp., possibly the residues from fodder,
and sedges.
Two other samples from the Church produced different and distinct assem-
blages. The fill (Context 75) of a pit excavated in the naos in 1993 contained
large numbers of mineralised fig (Ficus carica) and grape (Vitis vinifera) seeds.30
Another pit-fill in Trench A3-1 (Context 3) consisted of large amounts of car-
bonised sheep droppings, probably representing the residues from their use as
fuel.
Upper City, Trench L: The three assemblages from this trench were charac-
terised by a large proportion of cereal grains compared to the presence of chaff
and weed seeds. While the samples from the fills of a flue and one of the pithoi
(Contexts 343 and 360 respectively) produced small numbers of barley and
wheat grains, the sample from the fill (Context 346) of an oven yielded a very
large number of cereal grains which, although fragmentary, included free-thresh-
ing wheats, rachis fragments showing the presence of hexaploid naked wheats,
and barley, together with a small number of weed seeds (for example, Rumex
spp., Polygonum spp., and Bromus spp.).31 The dominance of cereal grains in
this sample suggests that these represent the residues from the advanced stages
of crop-processing.
Upper City, Trench TT: Eleven of the samples from this trench were scanned
and generally found to contain low concentrations of charred plant remains,
consisting mainly of cereal grains, very few rachis fragments and weed seeds.
Four samples were found to contain no identifiable plant remains. However,
many of the samples contained large quantities of charcoal. The range of cereals
represented was similar to the other trenches-free-threshing wheats, including
the hexaploid variety (identified on the basis of rachis fragments in several
samples), barley, rye and millet.
Only the sample from a pit-fill (Context 58) contained a relatively rich
assemblage, consisting of millet grains, together with a range of seeds represent-
ing potential weeds of cultivation. This included a large number of seeds of corn
gromwell (Lithospermum arvense).
The residues of other foodstuffs present in these samples included several
carbonised grape pips and a fragment of walnut (Juglans regia), recovered from
separate contexts.
Trench RD. Only four of the samples (from spits 1, 3, 6 and 9) have been
processed from this trench, and only spit 9 has produced a small quantity of
identifiable plant remains, including several wheat or barley grains, grape seeds
and a small number of weed seeds. The dry-sieved samples from these spits con-
tained large mammal bone fragments, plus varying quantities of pottery, build-
ing material and glass.
Discussion
The initial findings suggest that a range of cereal crops was used at
Amorium, together with their residues, which include the by-products from the
early as well as the latter stages of crop-processing-an indication that the crops
?AS XLIV (1994), 108.
31AS XLIV (1994), 112-3.
126

AMORIUM EXCAVATIONS 1994
were probably cultivated locally. The free-threshing wheats may well have been
used for human food, while the barley and millet may have served for both
human and animal consumption. Free-threshing wheats and barley were also the
best represented grains in the samples from the 1992 excavations. The evidence
for cereal production and, to a lesser extent, for viticulture fits well with the pic-
ture of agricultural activity in central Anatolia drawn from the seventh-century
Life of St. Theodore of Sykeon.32
A number of assemblages, particularly from the Triangular Tower, indicate
that part of the land in the vicinity of the city may well have been set aside for
the cultivation of hay crops, and, given the Byzantine context for the destruc-
tion, it is tempting to suggest that this was influenced by the needs of the mili-
tary forces stationed at Amorium.
The grapes, figs and walnut represented in the samples may have been
grown locally or imported to the site. All three species could have been used as
food (the figs and grapes in both fresh and dried form), while the grapes may
have been used for wine-making. Oil may have been extracted from the walnuts.
The assemblages from the Church highlight its later use as a farmhouse and
yard in the Seljuk period, consisting almost entirely of the residues from crop-
processing activities, with the inhabitants presumably utilising this material as
tinder. The large amounts of carbonised sheep droppings, found in this area
and also probably used as fuel, testify to the inhabitants' involvement in animal
husbandry. The predominance of cereal grains in the samples from Trench L
and Trench TT are indicative of the domestic activities associated with the final
cleaning of the grain.33
It is hoped that further processing and sorting of the samples will provide
more detailed information and further refine our picture of agricultural activities
at Amorium. The creation of a Siraf flotation tank on site should facilitate the
processing of larger samples for the recovery of environmental material. In addi-
tion, the analysis of the large quantity of animal bones recovered during excava-
tion will add important complementary evidence on the nature of animal hus-
bandry in the area.
CARVED STONE (Eric Ivison)
A considerable amount of work was done this year, with the welcome assis-
tance of Ramazan Alkan, Gorkem Feyzioglu, Lynda Mulvin, Simon Young and
Ipek Yticel. Almost 200 carved stones were inventoried during the 1994 season,
including stones found on survey, pieces still in situ in the Lower City Church
and fragments brought to the Stone Depot. The lion's share of the depot mater-
ial came from the Lower City Church. In the depot stones were sorted into
types, such as closure slabs and capitals, and were placed on shelves. Small frag-
ments were stored in trays for protection and easier access. The stone depot is
now becoming so full that some stones have been deposited in the pottery depot
next door. Massive stones which were less vulnerable to the weather, such as
column shafts, Roman gravestones and other large architectural members were
displayed outside in the Dig House garden. This collection has been arranged to
form the core of an open-air museum, and to this end a number of concrete

Carved Stones from the Lower City Church
Phase I. Late Antique fittings and furnishings
More fragments of Corinthian capitals, pilasters and closure slabs were cat-
alogued this year, but those found so far do not account for even a small per-
centage of those needed for the Phase I Basilica. Markings on the stylobate of
the nave colonnade suggest that twenty capitals were needed for the ground
floor, while many closure slabs would have been needed for the templon or as
barriers in any galleries or side aisles. Some fragments clearly have been looted
or destroyed in medieval or modern times, but the fate of many is explained by
an examination of the stones themselves. All the loose fragments were severely
broken up (no joining pieces have yet been found) and both carved and broken
surfaces were often covered with white lime mortar. This evidence indicates that
the fragments were reused as building material after they had been broken. This
conclusion is confirmed by the discovery of closure slab fragments built into the
walls of the Phase II Church. Other pieces were reversed and laid as part of the
opus sectile pavement, where their moulded impressions are now visible in the
mortar bed. One pier (Wall 34) also contains a fragment of a late fifth century
Corinthian capital. Indeed, the walls and piers of the second church are made
up almost exclusively of spolia, a large proportion of which probably came from
the Phase I Basilica. This material includes dressed limestone blocks, small
scraps of marble paving and revetment slabs, pieces of capitals and column
shafts, closure slabs and some decorative elements and furnishings. The north-
east central pier (Wall 55) also contains in situ a fragment of ambo parapet in
bluish-grey marble. This piece has the same mouldings as a parapet fragment
inventoried in 1993. Considering the reuse of much other Late Antique material
from the Basilica, it is surprising that the Corinthian capitals and closure slabs
were not reused. For it seems likely that, had they been intact, they would have
been incorporated into the new Church. This may suggest that the slabs and
capitals had already been so badly damaged by some form of catastrophe that
they could only be used as building material in the cores of the Phase II walls.
If so, then this offers some testimony to the wholescale destruction of the Phase I
Basilica, an event that required a complete rebuilding within the shell of its
outer walls. Who or what perpetrated this violence is not yet clear.
Phase II. Middle Byzantine fittings and furnishings
Closure slab fragment T614 was one of the most interesting and important
finds this year; it has now been deposited in Afyon Museum. The fragment was
found in loose rubble covering the opus sectile pavement in Trench Al towards
the western end of the naos. No other parts of this closure slab have been recov-
ered so far, but it is clear that it was carved from fine white marble, and that
both sides were worked to a high standard. On side A there was carved an eagle
in high relief (PI. XVI (b)), of which only the lower body is preserved. The eagle
is heavily stylised and is represented frontally against a plain background. Its
powerful upper legs and tail are carved with blade-shaped feathers arranged in
rows like overlapping scales. The feathers are textured with bold chisel strokes
and the quill at the centre of each feather is prominently shown. The eagle's
talons grasp an animal that lies fallen beneath the bird. The identity of the prey
128

AMORIUM EXCAVATIONS 1994
is not certain, but it is a quadruped with a shaggy coat, a tail and tall ears.
Reliefs of eagles are not uncommon in Middle Byzantine sculpture, and many
examples depict eagles seizing hares.34 The style of the Amorium slab, in partic-
ular the high relief and treatment of the feathers, compares well with sculptures
of eagles found in the North Church of the Monastery of Lips at
Constantinople (dedicated in 907).35 The reverse of the slab, side B, was carved
in shallow relief with stylised acanthus fronds divided by ribbons.
Many of the stones found in 1994 proved to be companion pieces or addi-
tional fragments of fittings and furnishings known from previous seasons. Such
a piece was T650, a large fragment of closure slab found in a later (Seljuk) rub-
bish pit in the bema, together with other important sculptures. Like T614, the
slab was of fine white marble and was carved on both sides in the Middle
Byzantine period. Side A (P1. XVII (a)) has the distinctive palmette motif
already noted on a number fragments from the church (slab T132 and templon
T477, phase 2), combined with the use of the twisted ribbon design. The other
face, side B (P1. XVII (b)), has a different design, carved with a crisply executed
design of twisted ribbons, filled with crosses and anthemia. This slab may be
recognised as a companion piece to two other closure slab fragments, T129 and
T471, both of which are carved from the same fine white marble with similar
designs to T650 on both sides. Although they have differing thicknesses and so
must come from separate slabs, it seems likely that all three belong to the same
templon. The decoration on the B sides of these slabs is reminiscent of, although
not as fully developed as, those of the templon in the katholikon of Hosios
Meletios, near Megara in Greece (dated c. 1100).36 The Amorium examples are
probably earlier, and perhaps a date in the middle decades of the eleventh cen-
tury would be appropriate.7 Another capital, T649, probably from a templon,
may also be added to this group. Although broken and in coarse grained
marble, this small capital appears to be a companion piece to T480, found in
1993.38 A number of octagonal colonette fragments in white limestone and
appropriate to a templon were also inventoried.
A large limestone block (P1. XVII (a)) that was found lining the same pit is
a non-joining portion of T486, also mentioned in the 1993 preliminary report.
The front of the block is carved with the same stylised acanthus design and
retains its red paint. The new block is better preserved than T486 and shows
that both are probably part of a decorative cornice rather than a templon, as
was previously suggested.39 Another two blocks of a decorative cornice noted in
previous seasons were found also this year. Several blocks are carved with a
twisted ribbon design and, like T245 uncovered in 1993, the new blocks still
have traces of paint.40

GLASS MOSAICS

were cleaned and conserved by Hande Guinyol (of the Istanbul Restorasyon ve
Konservasyon Merkez Laboratuvar), together with a large number of loose,
individual tesserae. Although the latter cannot be regarded as much more than
a representative sample since only in the Church was a sieve used to collect the
tesserae, it was decided to conduct a statistical survey of the material in order to
ascertain the proportion of types and their distribution across the site. As a
result, an inventory of more than 17,800 tesserae, almost entirely made of glass,
was compiled by the village hoca, Osman Aldemir. This has produced a number
of interesting general conclusions.

BRICK AND TILE (Karen Barker)
A catalogue of brick and tile, comprising 192 examples, has been compiled
and now forms the basis of a study collection of such material in the site depot.
They have been divided into five primary typological groups-(a) wall bricks,
(b) ridge tiles, (c) roof tiles, (d) floor tiles, and (e) miscellaneous items. These
have been further divided to smaller groups, largely on the basis of size. In addi-
tion, some examples have been distinguished by special features, such as a hole
pierced through the brick during manufacture or a production stamp.43 Others
display marks made accidentally before firing such as footmarks, both human
and animal, while others have a vitrified surface indicating anomalies in firing.
Finally, there are a few examples that show signs of later use as gaming boards.
Work so far has concentrated on classifying the numerous examples of wall
brick.
A large proportion of the material recovered during excavation belongs to
a type of brick that has been identified as one used principally in wall construc-
tion. Brick bonding courses can be observed in all three of the major buildings
as yet uncovered at the site-the Lower City Church, the Large Building and
the City Wall. The debris inside the Triangular Tower (Trench AB) has thus
been shown to be not roof collapse but part of the tower's superstructure.44
The
standard wall brick measures 0.34 m. (+ 0.01-0-02 m.) square and has a thick-
ness of 0-04-0-06 m. This may be regarded as equivalent to the pedialis of west-
ern use.45 The bricks are often decorated with simple designs, comprising finger-
groove patterns in straight or wavy lines. A few examples are pierced with a
hole, the purpose of which has yet to be explained.46
A second type is very similar but rectangular in shape, measuring 0-445 m.
by 0.325 m. (? 0015 m.) and again having a thickness of 0.04-0-06 m. This type
is not so common as the square variety, but both frequently have mortar adher-
ing to every surface. Another group is decorated with finger groove patterns
like those on the square and rectagular bricks but is trapezoidal in shape.47 A
final sub-group of wall bricks is made up of examples with dog-tooth protru-
sions. These would appear to have been made specially, but whether for decora-
tive or structural purposes remains uncertain.

Nineteen ridge tiles have been recorded but, since a complete example has
yet to be found, the overall length is unknown. They are, however, long and
narrow, rounded or semi-circular in section, and only 0015 m. (? 0.005 m.)
thick. None of them is decorated. These go together with a much larger group
of roof tiles, which are very fragmentary and, as a consequence, difficult to
classify, but they have an average thickness of less than 0.035 m. and often have
a lip at the edge or are slightly curved in shape.
Another small group has been tentatively been defined as floor tiles,
although these are difficult to distinguish from wall bricks and roof tiles. They
have been classified as such on the grounds that they are either extremely flat or
show signs of wear, paint or polish on the upper surface. It should be noted that
many of the floor tiles found in situ in the nave, narthex and north side aisles of
the Lower City Church are presumed to be reused wall bricks.48
COINS
Nineteen bronze or copper coins were recovered from the site during the
course of the season's excavations.49
Of these two Late Roman issues of the mid-
fourth century (SF2825 & SF2828) came from the Step Trench on the north side
of the Upper City, where a number of fifth-century coins had been found last
year. Three Byzantine coins (SF2956, SF2817 & SF2928) ranging from the sev-
enth to the eleventh century came from the new trench that extends the line of
the Step Trench southwards onto the top of the mound. Two eleventh-century
folles (SF2929 & SF2980), one a signed issue of Romanus IV (1068-1071), were
found in the vicinity of the gateway in Trench AB, together with a small,
unidentifiable coin (SF2969), possibly another nummus of the fifth century.
Three coins were also found in the Lower City Church. Significantly, one of
these was a Seljuk copper coin, found in a context belonging to the post-
Byzantine phase of occupation in the area of the bema (see below, p. 137-
Appendix, no. 1).50 The remaining eight coins were random surface finds.
For the first time a coin of the Roman imperial period has surfaced during
the excavations. The coin (SF2804) is a provincial issue from the mint at
Iconium, probably of the reign of Antoninus Pius (138-60).51 It is residual, how-
ever, having come from the rubble debris that lay above the floor at the west
end of the nave in the Lower City Church. Apart from this example, the earliest
coins are three issues of the fourth century, while there is one coin each from
the seventh and eighth centuries. By contrast, six coins date to the tenth-eleventh
centuries, of which an anonymous follis of Class Al (969-76) is the most signi-
ficant archaeologically since it was found on the surface layer immediately
below the collapse within the gateway of the Lower City walls. This at least sug-
gests that part of the fortifications was still standing at that date and confirms
the views expressed in previous reports.52
The most important numismatic finds of 1994, however, were the Islamic
coins. Previously only two examples had been recorded, whereas this season pro-

INSCRIPTIONS
No new inscriptions were found during the excavations in 1994, apart from
the small fragment from Trench AB and the cursive dipinto on a colonette capi-
tal, both of which have been referred to above. However, during the winter of
1994/5 an inscribed stele was found by one of the villagers, ;eraffetin Erdogan,
in his field across the stream to the north-west of the Lower City. The stele was
dug up together with some other large stones, which were immediately broken
up. The stele itself was apparently lying face-down in the ground, and the
inscription was only noticed when the stone was turned over. Although a more
detailed study and publication must wait until the coming season, it may be
noted here that the inscription records a vow by a certain Asclepiades to MT|rlp
'Optvi. This stele, therefore, forms a companion piece to the one recorded at
Hamzahacili in 1993 (and which is now safely stored in the Dig House depot)
and confirms the latter's original provenance as Amorium.55
For an inscription built into the mosque at Agllcik, see below.
REMOTE-SENSING SURVEY
In addition to the Conservation Assessment Survey of the Lower City
Church, the other major initiative and innovation of the 1994 season was the use
of the British Institute's balloon to conduct an aerial photographic survey of the
site. This was carried out between August 2 and 7, and we greatly benefitted
from the help provided by Keith Jordan from the Ankara office. The blimp was
filled with a mixture of helium and air and, although this meant that lift was
therefore limited and shots were taken at low altitude (normally 50 metres), it
gave greater stability and control over its use, especially in breezy conditions.
The results of survey are still being analysed, but they constitute a valuable
photographic record not only of the unexcavated sectors of the site
(PI. XVIII (b)) but also of the present state of the trenches (Pls. XIII (b) and
XIX (a)). In addition, outlying features, notably the quarries and the necropolis
containing several monumental tombs and small tumuli to the south and west of

REGIONAL SURVEY
During the course of the survey considerable evidence was gathered for set-
tlement patterns and population density in the territory of Amorium. Many
Phrygian doorstones, both intact and fragmentary, which do not figure in the
standard works, were recorded in the villages to the south of Hisar.56 Two
doorstones, for example, were noted near the village of Demircili, in Ozbekli
mahallesi; another stone from the same place was removed illegally some years
ago and is now said to be in the Eskigehir Museum. Two fine inscribed examples
were also seen in the village of Tezkoy, while another inscription, kindly shown
to us in the garden of one of the villagers, appears to be an honorific altar or
statue base set up by the people of Appola to lulia Domna. The quantity and
quality of the funerary material attest to the prosperity of the rural population
in Roman times. In addition, an isolated site at (aydere near Sogukkuyu would
appear to have been a Roman-late antique farmhouse on the evidence of the
surface pottery scatter. Earlier occupation was attested by two important mound
sites-Tezkoy
hoyuik, to the north-west of the village, where an abundance of
red-burnished fine wares suggests a major second millennium B.C. Hittite settle-
ment, and Kirkel hoyuk near Cayki?lar, which produced large quantities of first
millennium B.C. Phrygian-type wares.57
It was, however, the richness and variety of the Byzantine remains that was
particularly striking. These included three rock-cut church sites (Akgam, Inonti,
and Biiyiikkale Arkasi mevkii), two other possible churches (one at Manasir
Tepe, the other at Hacilar, where a modern Turkish tiirbe includes several fine
templon fragments), the spectacular refuge site at E?yahasan (E?ek Asar) and
the fort/watch-tower complex on the summit of Boztepe, which commands an
excellent view of Amorium and the rolling plains to the north. These sites, and
the scattered architectural fragments that are to be found reused in buildings
and cemeteries everywhere, attest to the important role played by the rugged
hinterland that lies to the south of Amorium in supporting and sustaining the
Byzantine thematic capital. One recalls the story of how the future emperor Leo
III, then commander of the Anatolic theme, sent the bishop of Amorium for
safety into the mountains during the troubles in 716.58
Carved Stones from the 1993 and 1994 Surveys
The regional survey has already registered a large number of carved stones
of all types-architectural, sculptural and epigraphic. Some were built into pri-
vate houses and agricultural buildings, but many had been incorporated into
important public structures such as mosques, fountains, cemeteries and other
monuments (notably tirbeler). Other stones, however, were found simply lying
in village streets or open fields, and it is to be hoped that the 1994 material will

Probably the earliest of the stones in date is a relief carved from local pur-
plish-brown andesite, found with fragments of andesite blocks in a field at Altar
mevkii near Tiirkmen k6y. The relief (P1. XIX (b)) is on a rectangular block and
depicts a standing female figure, carved in low relief. Her pose is frontal, with a
long skirt covering her body while her left arm is folded across her waist. On her
head is a solar disc, which may identify her as a goddess. The style and material
of the relief suggest a Neo-Hittite or Phrygian date (c. 1200-600 B.c.). The dis-
covery of a Neo-Hittite relief in the area is not unprecedented, for a basalt
orthostat decorated in relief with a griffin-headed figure was found in 1950 close
to the village of Daydali, only 5 km. to the east of Amorium.59
Roman, Late Antique and Middle Byzantine material made up the majority
of the pieces recorded during the surveys. Perhaps the finest architectural piece
from the 1993 survey was an impost fragment (P1. XX (a)) that would probably
have surmounted a Corinthian capital. Only half of the impost is preserved, but
both the carving and the marble is of high quality. All three of the surviving
sides are carved in relief with stiff acanthus fronds, which on the front and back
faces frame respectively a Latin cross and a wreath containing a monogramatic
cross. The impost belongs to the same, so-called "Theodosian" type found in
the Basilica of the Acheiropoitos at Thessaloniki (c. 460-470) and the Basilica
of St. John Studios at Constantinople, constructed in the 450s.60 The closest
parallels at Amorium for the style of this impost are the fragments of
Corinthian and composite capitals from the Lower City Church, which may be
dated to the latter part of the fifth century.61 A number of Ionic impost capitals
carved of bluish-grey marble were also recorded, most of which were of the
same type as examples found in the excavations. They all probably date to the
late fifth and sixth centuries.62
Epistyles from templa featured heavily amongst the Middle Byzantine
carved stones encountered on the surveys. Dates for these stones can only be
approximate, but they fit well into the general picture of liturgical furniture
known from this area of Anatolia of the tenth and eleventh centuries.
Inscription. Epitaph of Pientios
This important inscription (P1. XX (b)) commemorates in verse (elegiac coup-
lets) a bishop called Pientios (presumably of Amorium), whose tomb was set up
by his son, Eusebios. It was first noted by Professor Cyril Mango in 1962, but
the text has never been published in full.6

"The heavenly halls hold in spirit the wise arch-priest Pientios within their
gates, but this small tomb guards the remains of his flesh until he tastes the
food of the resurrection. Little Eusebios has set up this small memorial in
honour of his father, repaying a little of his immeasurable kindness."
CONCLUSION
It should be obvious that the benefits which accrue from conducting the
survey for the Turkish Ministry of Culture are manifold. In addition to con-
tributing to the national register of sites and monuments that is presently being
compiled by the Department of Monuments and Museums, the Amorium
Project itself gains immeasurably by being able to set the excavations in their
wider historical and geographical context.
Likewise, despite the limited funds available in 1994, the excavations have
produced a wealth of results. New evidence has come to light both on the Upper
City mound and at the Lower City Church for the continued existence of the
settlement in the post-Byzantine era. The archaeological remains reflect the
decline of the site under the Seljuks and its transformation from an important
Byzantine fortified city to a rustic backwater by the time that the Ottomans
took control of the region. It is to be hoped that these material findings can
be combined with documentary evidence, historical records and demographic
studies to furnish an unique picture of the life of a rural community in central
Anatolia during the late medieval period. But they have already provided an
intriguing glimpse into medieval life in the borderlands between the Byzantine
and Seljuk empires-one that is rather surprising, for it apparently reveals one
of a peaceful semi-rural existence, quite different from the turbulent history that
Amorium had previously endured as a major Byzantine city.
The Church remains the show-piece of the excavations. Not only is it an
impressive monument to visit, but also the recent work has highlighted its
unique character in terms of both its radical conversion from a basilica into a
cross-domed basilica church and the richness of its ecclesiastical furnishings and
decoration. Finally, the 1994 season has laid the foundations for a more inten-
sive study of the pattern of urban settlement at Amorium, and it is to be hoped
that this will lead to a better understanding of the city's development during the
crucial period of the Byzantine Dark Age.

Casts of the five coins found during the 1994 season, together with those of two
coins from earlier seasons, were studied. Of these, four were immediately identi-
fiable. They form a small group representing three members of the Rum Seljuk
dynasty and bear witness to the existence of a local monetary economy at
Amorium in the first half of the thirteenth century. The most interesting and
unusual coin is the last in the catalogue; it appears to have been double-struck.
The two other coins found in 1994 have yet to be identified, and it is hoped that
personal examination of the actual coins in Afyon will resolve the present un-
certainty. One, however, is clearly Islamic (SF2867), perhaps an early Ottoman
copper coin, but the other (SF2829) is more problematic. The remaining coin
(SF81), found in 1988, is also thought to be a thirteenth century Seljuk issue.65
