By H. D. BAKER, D. COLLON, J. D. HAWKINS, T. POLLARD,
J. N. POSTGATE, D. SYMINGTON and D. THOMAS
1. THE SITE AND ITS SETTING
(J. N. Postgate)
The G6ksu valley (Fig. 1), which furnishes one of the two main routes from
Turkey's southern shore through the Taurus to the Konya plain and the rest of
the central plateau, must be deemed one of the country's national treasures. The
clear turquoise ribbon of the river threads its way south-eastwards from high in
the mountains west of Ermenek to reach the Mediterranean at Silifke, or rather
at the mouth of the delta it has created to the south of the classical and modern
town. The valley itself, however, is by no means uniform, nor is the road which
follows it. Coming from the interior, the traveller leaves the Konya plain not far
south of Karaman, and after a gentle climb to the pass at Sertavul, begins to
descend thickly pine-grown slopes high on the eastern shoulders of the valley,
with the G6ksu itself glimpsed occasionally flowing far below. Dropping steeply
down, leaving the monastery of Alahan high above one to the left, one passes
into a very different landscape around the regional centre of Mut, Roman
Claudiopolis (earlier Ninica, see Mitchell 1979). Here the G6ksu is joined from
the west by the Ermenek Su, and the narrow valleys of their upper courses give
way to a wide, low-lying basin, where the stream has cut through one terrace
after another, to yield a curiously desert landscape in which remaining patches
of the geological terraces, harbouring olive groves and cereal crops in early sum-
mer, stand isolated from one another by high eroded scarps, whose steep bare
yellow and white limestone slopes are studded only sparsely with pines and low
evergreen bushes like prickly oak. The river has trenched so deep that most of
the terrain lies below the 1000 ft (= 325 m.) contour, and the G6ksu itself near
the site is at less than 100 m. above sea level. Consequently, it is not surprising
that on the climatic map of Turkey the G6ksu basin shares with Adana and
Mersin in the Cukurova the privilege of being in the country's highest tempera-
ture zone in July and August (Yeni Tfrkiye Atlasi, Ankara 1977). Similarly, the
annual rainfall is low, less than 500 mm., and in July, August and September
less than 5 mm. of rain a month is recorded.
A major barrage is planned for the lower G6ksu. A dam of 70 m. height is
designed to serve for flood control and hydroelectric power. The site of the dam
is just south-east of the modern bridge on which the road from Silifke to Mut
crosses to the left bank of the river, some 10 km. from Silifke and 7 km. down-
stream from Degirmendere. Information from government engineers is that the
maximum water level of the planned barrage will be in the region of 150 m.
above sea level, and this would completely submerge the site of Kilise Tepe.
As a consequence of the physical geography, the human exploitation of this
landscape is intimately related to the hydrology (Fig. 2). The G6ksu itself flows
too low to supply irrigation, except by motor pumps to a limited area along one
bank or the other; but along the affluents like the Kurtsuyu there has been
extensive water management, with substantial channels taking off some kilo-
metres upstream, and watering a broad triangle of orchards and other crops
down to the banks of the G6ksu itself. Elsewhere, at several levels, there are
natural springs. At Kilise Tepe itself one permanent spring has been piped, and
there are at least two minor springs left in their natural state nearby, though for
Killa village, south of the mound on the left bank of the G6ksu, the water
source is now some distance away to the north of the main road. Some 5 km. to

This spring has religious status, and a reputed link to the mosque and tomb of
Seyh Ali Semerkandi at Zeyne itself, and just above Zeyne a connection between
water and worship is suggested by the fact that the modern water supply for the
town comes from the hill of Ala Kilise, where a Byzantine church was noted by
Heberdey in 1896 and visited and described in 1908 by Guyer (1909-10).1
Considering the wealth and strategic importance of the Goksu valley, sur-
prisingly few archaeological sites have been reported from here. For prehistoric
times there are only Kilise Tepe (under the name of Maltepe), Cingene Tepe
(C(ingantepe) across the river, At Tepe (also Artepe) west of Mut at the conflu-
ence of the Goksu and the Ermenek Su, Mut itself, and Orentepe near Mut
(French 1965, 179 f.). As on the Mediterranean coast in this part of Turkey, the
most prolific period is the Byzantine. Quite apart from Kilise Tepe itself, Ala
Kilise, Maltepe proper which may be at least partly Byzantine, and the
monastery of Alahan, already mentioned, there are several other locations in
the Mut region where Byzantine architecture and pottery have been noted.2
However, in this hilly country ancient sites naturally protrude less and are more
liable to be removed entirely by the effects of rainfall, and we must expect that
detailed surveys on foot would reveal other sites. Indeed, as indicated on Fig. 2,
we have already observed sherd scatters at three locations near to Kilise Tepe,
and some of the sherds are pre-classical.
Whether the valley served at all times as a principal route, as one would
tend to assume from its modern role, is perhaps not entirely certain. The pres-
ence of the rock relief at Keben at about the halfway point between Kilise Tepe
and the coast, points to a Late Bronze Age usage, but the Roman documenta-
tion rather suggests that in those times the major route was more to the west,
striking north past Meydancikkale to reach the plateau in the Bey?ehir region.
According to the map in Mitford 1980, before p. 1233, the route from Silifke
kept to the hills, passing through Pracana (= ?), and only rejoined the river to
cross it just below Mut; and one notes that a similar route through the hills was
followed by Heberdey at the end of the last century.3 Nevertheless, the Goksu
route could never have been totally abandoned, and a Roman milestone has
recently been reported from the region of Yenisu on the Mut-Silifke road about
25 km north of Silifke.4
Kilise Tepe

than 30 years, under the name of "Maltepe". It was first mentioned in print by
Mellaart: "a most impressive site situated on a ridge in the plain overlooking
the river (G6ksu) and with a good spring of water gushing from the foot of the
rock. Second millennium pottery is as abundant as that of the third and first"

(1958, 315). Subsequently it was again surveyed by French (1965), when empha-
sis was laid on the 2nd and 3rd millennium pottery. On our arrival at the site,
we noted that the local population refer to it as Kilise Tepe, and that there is a
site known to them as Maltepe on the other bank of the G6ksu, about 4 km. to
the west. Later we discovered that these are also the names given on the official
1:25,000 map of the area, and close reading of the account of Heberdey makes
it clear that the name of Maltepe was already given to the western site 100 years
ago (Heberdey & Wilhelm, 1896). It was therefore agreed with the Directorate
of Monuments and Museums that the name of Kilise Tepe should henceforth be
used for the site we are excavating, reserving Maltepe for the correct mound of
that name.
Hittite burnished sherds retrieved from the surface of the site suggested that
it lay within the borders of the Late Hittite empire for a while, more specifically
in the territory of the Lower Lands. As described by Hawkins below, the bronze
treaty tablet and other epigraphical evidence have recently combined to shed
new light on the political status of these southern parts of the Hittite lands. It is
clear that direct control, whether from Hattusa or Tarhuntassa, extended to the
sea coast at Silifke (whether or not this is the location of Ura, cf. Beal 1992),
and considerably further to the west at Perge. This is only to be expected at an
era when Cyprus (Alasiya) fell under Hittite domination; but it creates an
expectation that during the later part of the Late Bronze Age Kilise Tepe would
have functioned as an administrative centre and no doubt military garrison of
the Hittite kings.
There is little to be said about the political status of the region at other his-
torical periods. In the Middle Bronze Age Assyrian traders were surely active at
Kara Hyuiik (Konya) as well as Acem H6yiuk on the central plateau, whether or
not they passed through western Cilicia to use the Goksu valley. In the Iron Age
the only historical references to the Silifke area (if we exclude obscure traditions
transmitted by classical sources) come from the military campaigns of the
Assyrian kings and especially of Neriglissar (see Hawkins, below). Our district
falls within western Cilicia, and it is worth noting that the Assyrian governor of
Que under Sargon in the late eighth century was involved in exchanges between
the Phrygian and Assyrian courts, as well as engaged in relations with the minor
kings of Tabal: his routes of communication seem likelier to have run through
the Cilician gates than up the Calycadnus, and it is impossible to guess the
political status of the valley in these centuries before we hear of the mountain
states centered at Olba (Diocaesarea) to the east and Meydancikkale to the
west.
2. THE HISTORICAL GEOGRAPHY OF SOUTH CENTRAL ANATOLIA (J. D. Hawkins)
The historical geography of the south coast of Asia Minor is comparatively
simple to describe. In the east lay the Cilician plain, Cilicia Pedias, sharply
demarcated to the north and west by the vast sweep of the Taurus Mountains.
This chain forms the great central projection of the south coast, where the
mountains fall steeply into the sea along a wide front, affording few natural har-
bours or, on land, routes of communication. This is historically Rough Cilicia
(Tracheia). To its west is the smaller coastal plain of Pamphylia, and beyond
that a further knot of mountains forming the south-west projection, Lycia. The
main feature of the rugged landscape of Rough Cilicia is the deep-cut valley of
the Calycadnus (G6ksu) river, running north-west to south-east and offering one
of the few routes from the central plateau through the Taurus mountains to the
coast.

Rough Cilicia in classical times lived up to its name, and was notoriously
difficult to control, being the home of mountain brigands and pirates, and
no doubt this character extended back into more remote antiquity. Certainly
neither the Neo-Babylonian nor the Assyrian kings, who controlled Que (Plain
Cilicia), ever ventured more than an occasional foray into the western moun-
tains. We have the curiosity of a quite detailed account of a campaign here by
the Neo-Babylonian king, Neriglissar (who calls the country Pirindu), from
557 B.C.5 Retaliating to aggression from its king Appuasu, Neriglissar invaded
and crossed the difficult terrain, sacking cities, including Ura' and Kirsu, as far
as the Lydian frontier. The Assyrian kings knew Rough Cilicia as Hilakku,
agreed to be the origin of the Greek terms Kilikes and Kilikia,6 but only the
most energetic encountered the people. Shalmaneser III claimed tribute from
the king along with other rulers in 858 B.C.,7 and Sargon II seems to have exer-
cised some control in the period c. 718-713 B.C.8 Sennacherib's generals put
down a Hilakku-led revolt in western Que in 696 B.C.,9 and Esarhaddon con-
ducted a punitive raid in 679 B.C.l? Later Assurbanipal had diplomatic relations
with its king Sandasarme." The Luwian dynast Azatiwata, who controlled
Adana and the Cilician plain, speaks of "bad men, brigands, who did not serve
under Mopsos's house" and of "strong fortresses in the west", all of which he
subdued.12 This doubtless refers to the people of Hilakku, and the incidents may
date to the reign of Sennacherib and connect with the events of his Que cam-
paign.l3 Such references serve to establish the character of the country in the
early Iron Age.
For the period of the Hittite Empire (Late Bronze Age), the geography of
the south coast has been much clarified by recent discoveries. The bronze tablet
discovered in 1986, bearing the treaty between Tudhaliya IV of Hatti and his
first cousin, Kurunta king of Tarhuntassa,'4 contains among other items a
detailed description of the new frontiers of the latter's kingdom,15 a revised
version of the long-known frontier of the "Hulaya River Land" in KBo IV, 10.
The kingdom, which was already known to include an area on the plateau
between Konya and the Taurus, extended at this time from Kizzuwatna (Plain
Cilicia) in the east16 as far as Parha and the Kastaraya River (Perge on the
Kestros)l7 in the west. The fact that the early king Telipinu included the Hulaya
River Land among his store-cities (see below), supports Garstang's conclusion
that this river was on the plateau and is to be identified with the C(ar?amba Cay
system,'8 which rises in Lake Bey?ehir and flows out into the marshes on the

Tarhuntassa, land and city, are not known to have existed before the reign
of Muwatalli II, who apparently founded the city, moving his capital there and
abandoning Hattusa.19 Subsequently his son and successor Urhi-Tesub moved
the capital back to Hattusa,20 but when Muwatalli's brother Hattusili usurped
the throne, he reestablished Tarhuntassa, installing as king Kurunta, another
son of Muwatalli,21 who had grown up in his own household,22 and regulating
his position by treaty.23 Kurunta was still ruling here when Tudhaliya IV suc-
ceeded his father Hattusili and issued a new treaty with Tarhuntassa, that pre-
served on the bronze tablet. This latter treaty, while emphasizing the loyalty and
love between Tudhaliya and Kurunta, contains a whole series of concessions to
the latter over against the original treaty of Hattusili, and this perhaps hints at
the beginning of stress between the two countries. Impressions of seals of
Kurunta recently excavated at Bogazkoy24 give him the royal aedicula of titles
borne otherwise only by the Hittite Great King, and show that he had claims to
the supreme position of his father against the usurping line of his uncle.
However relations between Tudhaliya and Kurunta ended, in the subse-
quent generation there is new evidence for open warfare between Hatti and
Tarhuntassa. The SUDBURG Hieroglyphic inscription, excavated at Bogazkoy in
1988,25 contains a historical account by Tudhaliya's son Suppiluliuma II,26 the
last known king of Hattusa, of a campaign against Lukka lands and
Tarhuntassa resulting in their conquest.

If Muwatalli was indeed the founder of Tarhuntassa, we should enquire
what is known of the area before the 13th century B.C. The answer is-nothing
from the historical sources. The Hittites certainly had a strong interest in the
Konya plain, their "Lower Land", from the very beginning of their realm. They
reckoned the first expansion of their forefather Labarna to be in this direction,
for he is recorded to have conquered and held cities certainly located here:
Hupisna, Tuwanuwa, Nenassa, Lanta, Zallara, Parsuhanta and Lusna.29 What
this seems to imply is that the Hittites held the Lower Land by a ring of garri-
son cities around its southern periphery against the difficult mountain terrain
beyond. It is likely that their control of this area was generally maintained in
normal times. Telipinu's broken list of store-cities30
includes places known to be
here, among them Ikkuwaniya (= Konya), Hurniya and Parsuhanda, also the
Hulaya river. A notable interruption of this control occurred in the crisis known
as the "concentric invasions" during the reign of Suppiluliuma's father
Tudhaliya III, when the Arzawan enemy invaded the Lower Land to make
Tuwanuwa and Uda his frontier.31 But such an event seems to have been excep-
tional. When from the time of Suppiluliuma the Hittite state incorporated
Kizzuwatna, it does not seem likely that control of the Lower Land would have
been seriously disturbed.
There is no indication however that any Hittite king, either of the Old
Kingdom or of the early Empire, ever before the time of Muwatalli attempted
serious control of the Taurus mountains south of the Lower Land. We may thus
view Muwatalli's foundation of Tarhuntassa as a radical policy departure, no
less than a move to incorporate this great chunk of mountainous terrain, later
Rough Cilicia, under direct rule of the Hittite Empire by the expedient of plant-
ing a new Hittite capital city in its midst. Even after the move of the capital
back to Hattusa, Hattusili recognized the importance of control of this area by
reestablishing Tarhuntassa as a sub-kingdom under his nephew. Further evi-
dence suggests, as noted above, that subsequently the sub-kingdom grew into a
very real threat to the metropolis.
Where then might the site of the city Tarhuntassa be sought? As already
mentioned, the Hulaya River, which gave its name to at least part of the king-
dom, was clearly on the plateau; it was almost certainly the Carsamba Cay, as
already seen by Garstang in 1956 (above, n. 18). By the time of Hattusili how-
ever, the Calycadnus valley would have formed the actual heart of the kingdom
of Tarhuntassa. It probably constituted both its main agricultural base and its
key route from the plateau to the sea. One might thus begin to seek
Tarhuntassa at some controlling position in the valley. But the question is also
bound up with the location of the city Ura, known as an important port on the
Anatolian coast facing Ugarit. This coast as noted is by no means rich in
natural harbours.
Ura has usually been located in the neighbourhood of Silifke at the mouth
of the Calycadnus, which would fit well with the location of Tarhuntassa further
up the same valley. Recently however Beal has argued for the location of Ura at
Gilindere, largely on the basis of the description of the march to Ura in the

3. THE SITE AND ITS SURVEY (T. Pollard)
The survey was carried out by T. Pollard and S. Blakeney between 23/7/94
and 11/8/94. We had use of a Sokkia Set 5 total station with Psion II datalogger
running Toposet. Initial processing of the data was carried out by T. Pollard in
SDR Mapping and Design, running on an IBM ThinkPad 750Cs. Contour plans
at 1 m. and at 2 m. intervals were produced using SURFER, working on data
converted to ASCII within SDR Map; see Fig. 3. The origin of our site grid was
located at an arbitrary point to the south-west of the mound, so that the south
and east sides of the laid-out grid should enclose the entire mound. The origin
was numbered (1000 m. east, 1000 m. north) so that, should a wider survey of
the surrounding area be undertaken, it would all fall within the same (positively-
numbered) grid.
The south and east sides of the grid were laid out with iron pins at 50 m.
intervals. The west side was not laid out as this would have entailed placing pins
in the middle of a group of migrant workers' huts. Two baselines completed, the
50 x 50 m. grid was taken over the mound. From the top of the mound, we set
out a 10 X 10 m. grid within the 50 x 50 m. over those parts of the site (i.e. the
top) which we intended to clear or excavate.
Although the 4-figure numerical coordinates of each grid point were
known, it was decided for practical purposes of every-day recording that each
10 x 10 m. square on the site should be assigned a short-hand reference com-
posed of letters for the eastings and numbers for the northings, originating from
the south-west corner of the site. Thus the south-west corner of the excavated
square K20 was at coordinates 1100, 1200.
The contour survey of the mound itself was recorded on an approximate
5 m. grid, which was thought adequate to record the level of detail desired. Off
the mound measurements were taken less regularly, with the aim of simply
recording the salient features of the immediate surroundings and their contours.
Our temporary site datum was located at 1150,1150 on the site grid, and given
the nominal height of 100 m., in relation to which all this year's heights are
recorded. From the 1:25,000 map it is clear that this in fact lies at approximately
142 m. above sea level, though this remains to be confirmed.
The mound and its environs
Kilise Tepe occupies the largest of a row of flat-topped conglomerate-
capped bluffs which look westwards over the Kurtsuyu and Goksu river valleys,
approximately 50 m. above the present river level. The bluffs run along a
roughly north-south line for about 1.5 km.: ours is at the centre of this line
(Plate XXI (a)).
The bluffs vary somewhat in character. To the north of the site is a 20 m.
high promontory which has a distinct conglomerate layer on top, and large
148

KILISE TEPE 1994
quantities of fallen boulders littering its sides. Yet its eastern part has an accu-
mulation of 1 m. of soil on its top and is therefore cultivable. According to the
villagers, stone-capped graves have been encountered on the south side of this
bluff where it faces Kilise Tepe, but we have not investigated this claim in detail.
Beyond Kilise Tepe to the south, but north of the village of Ki?la, are several
much less imposing outcrops on which the conglomerate cap is very indistinct,
but which are nonetheless uncultivated because the conglomerate is too close to
the surface. I believe that our promontory was originally similar to the northern
bluff, on account of its similar height and breadth, its intermittently distinct
conglomerate layer, and the scatter of large boulders strewn round its flanks.
The land around the site is given over entirely to agriculture (wheat, olives,
vines and fruit trees). Ploughing continues right up to any significant change in
the slope gradient (i.e. to the top edges and bottoms of the bluffs) except in
those cases mentioned above. The multitude of trees, particularly to the west of
the site, are supported by the numerous springs in the area, two of which fall in
the survey area, supplemented by irrigation from further upstream. These must
also have provided for the inhabitants of the settlement.
The promontory on which our site sits measures nearly 200 m. north-south
at the level of the conglomerate layer, and on its western side is some 16 m.
higher than the gently sloping land below it. With the mound rising 10-12 m.
from this base, and measuring 120 by 100 m. at the top, the illusion of grandeur
when viewed from the valley is quite unmistakable. The top of the mound is not
absolutely flat-the northern half is about 1-1.5 m. higher than the southern
half, and from a distance there is a noticeable "step" between them. The north-
ern half is the reported location of the Byzantine church which gave the mound
its name. The mound is almost perilously steep on all but its eastern side, which
has a gentle concave curve to it. There are minor variations in the gradients of
the slopes on all sides, some of which may relate to buried archaeological
features. However, a slight ridge which ran across the northern end of the
mound proved on excavation to have no structural, or other apparent basis, and
it seems likely that most or all of the variations are due to erosional processes.
At the south-east corner of the mound, where the wash apron meets the edge of
the promontory, there are several extremely large slabs of conglomerate, some
up to 10 m. in length, which appear to be arranged to form a series of ramps.
While excavation is obviously necessary to determine whether this is in fact the
case, there is the possibility that this may have been associated with an access
route up to the mound.
4. SURFACE COLLECTION
(David C. Thomas)
The surface collection and clearance of the mound was undertaken with the
aim of analysing the distribution of ceramic sherds, stones and artefacts lying on
the surface, and of recording the surface marks to discover if any correlations
exist between these remains of human activity, surface marks and sub-surface
features.
A grid was set up around the mound and 10 x 10 m. squares were laid out
over the top of the mound, lettered (H-T) from West to East and numbered
(9-22) from South to North. Before beginning the collection in each square, the
supervisor sketched any surface marks, depressions, slopes, etc. onto a scrape
sheet, at the scale of 1:100. Also recorded on the scrape sheet were the date,
square number, unit number and any general comments about the square. These
included a rough appraisal of the density of the stone coverage in the square-
light, medium or dense-and a comment on the vegetation, where noteworthy.
149

ANATOLIAN STUDIES
A process of trial and error revealed that the most efficient way of conduct-
ing the surface collection was to have 5 to 6 workmen and a supervisor in a
square. The surface material was scraped into small piles and then sorted on to
sacks, to ease its subsequent transferral into wheelbarrows. All ceramic sherds
were collected in labelled baskets and then sent to be washed. The stones were
divided into piles of large stones (> 15 cm.) and small stones (< 15 cm.) and any
carefully worked or decorated pieces were kept. The large stones were then
counted and the quantities of large and small stones recorded in numbers of
level wheelbarrow loads. Initially, all unusual stones and objects were collected
and separately numbered; many of these were later discarded as amorphous
lumps of rock, but the procedure encouraged the workmen to be observant.
Before each pile of stones was emptied into the wheelbarrow, the supervisor
checked through it for any missed sherds and artefacts (Plate XXI (b)). The
workmen were generally quick to recognize sherds, but occasionally missed
larger pieces of pottery and fragments of tiles. On a good day, a team of work-
men could clear the surface of 10 squares.
In total 129 squares were treated in this manner, and showed a wide range
of stone density. The northern part of the mound and the edges tended to have
a light stone coverage, few potsherds and little vegetation. These squares were
quick to clear and could be scraped very clean. Other areas of the mound had
very dense stone coverage and were much slower to clear: the large stones were
concentrated on the west and southern parts of the mound, especially in squares
116-18, J16-17 and K16. K16 alone had over 600 large stones, while another
had 19 wheelbarrow loads of small stones. The squares with the densest stone
coverage were concentrated in the central eastern part of the mound (O-R,
13-16), with squares of medium stone coverage being found in the west and
south. There were smaller concentrations on the eastern part, in squares R18
and S18. Small stones were most plentiful on the western and south-eastern
upper slopes but the highest concentration was in squares P15 and Q15, nearer
the centre of the mound, from which a total of 39 barrow loads were removed.
Dressed stone was quite common in some squares and there seemed to be a
rough positive correlation between high stone density and the occurrence of
Byzantine tiles. A large number of tiles were recorded and kept for future study.
Their distribution from squares I to Q (west to east) and 19 to 15 (north to
south) presumably indicates that the site of the Byzantine church, which gave
the mound its name, is to be found within these limits, possibly in the western
part where the large stones were concentrated, with another large building
(probably not stone built) to the east, at the centre of the mound. The fragments
of grooved drain-pipes forming a grid on a west to east alignment between
squares 115 and P15 and on north-south alignments between K19-15 and
P16-14 would have been well-placed to service such a building complex but their
date still remains to be established.
Analysis of the sketches of the surface marks revealed some interesting pat-
terns. Marks interpreted as furrows were generally found on the centre and
south of the mound, while wall marks were most common on the north of the
mound. Field boundaries (coinciding roughly with those shown on the official
cadastral map) were marked on the ground by shallow ditches, and associated
low banks. One of these, which runs roughly east-west across the middle of the
mound, delineates the ploughed and unploughed halves of the mound. A clear
stone- and vegetation-free strip seems to run round much of the edge of the
mound and may be negative evidence of a wall, which has since been removed.
Several unavoidable factors affected the consistency of the surface collec-
150

KILISE TEPE 1994
tion. Different supervisors and workmen inevitably differed in their practices
and thoroughness, but the general procedure and recording technique applied
across the mound was designed to compensate for this. The greater the stone
density, the harder it was to clean the surface and to maintain concentration.
Dense thistle coverage hampered the recovery of ceramics and small artefacts,
while the time of day and position of the sun affected the visibility of surface
marks. In the view of the overall supervisor, however, the effects of these vari-
ables were minimal and the fact that some of the surface marks join up suggests
that they may be significant, as they were noted independently, a square at a
time, and not in a sweep across the mound.
5. THE EXCAVATIONS
(J. N. Postgate)
Excavation was started in three main areas: at the north-west end of the
mound in squares H-K20, on the eastern edge in squares Q19 and R18, and
in a row of three 5 m. soundings (K14, N12 and Q10) placed from NW to SE
diagonally across the south half of the tepe (see Fig. 3). In each of these three
soundings the results were similar, in that each revealed stone built architecture
standing a few courses high and filled with remarkably loose dry soil. Walls did
not have plastered faces, or distinctive mortar, the floors were only of earth,
and we did not observe a build-up of levels in association with any of the walls.
Apart from a few hearths and large jars which had remained in situ, there was
very little of note in these squares. Only in Q10 did the stone constructions give
way lower down to mud-brick which we suspect represents the top of pre-
Classical deposits.
The Eastern side (Fig. 4; Plate XXII (a))
The principal feature on the eastern side is a massive stone wall (Wall 300)
which runs north-south along the rim of the mound. This measures 2.45 m. in
width in squares Q-R19, i.e. about 30-40 m south of the northern end of the
tepe. The wall was traced over a total length of about 25 m., and stands in
places 3 courses (1.2 m.) high: it is not preserved north of Q20, and it was
followed for about 5 m. southwards into Q18. The inner face is continuous and
approximately straight, but to north and south of Q-R19 the outer face is not
well preserved and the width of the masonry is much less. It is possible that the
extra thickness of the wall in Q-R19 is the result of a tower or gate construc-
tion, but further work will be needed to test this.
Supplying a date for this wall is difficult, since there are no firmly associ-
ated stratified deposits against either face. The clearest indicator is a hearth
floor of clay over pebbles, which lies directly beneath the masonry of the wall at
the south end of the exposed stretch in R18. This belongs to a type well repre-
sented in the highest stratified level in K20 at the north end, of Late Bronze or
Early Iron date. Beneath this our excavation in R18, outside the line of the wall,
exposed horizontal stratified deposits between +100-00 and +99-00 m. These can
hardly have been laid down except at a time when the settlement extended east-
wards beyond the line of the present rim of the mound, implying that the site's
eastern edge was already eroded at the time the main wall was built. We do not
have sufficient stratified pottery from these layers to date them confidently,
because they were cut into by at least three well formed circular pits (diameters
of two being 1 80 m. and 2 10 m.), each containing finely layered deposits. These
pits are quite different in nature from those in the K20 area, to be described
later; they seem to have been filled by a gradual accumulation of fine strata,
with occasional sherds and ashy debris, but their purpose remains obscure. The

(2) Pit 94/44: a 70 cm. deep circular stone-lined pit of 2-20 m. diameter with
sides gradually narrowing to the base. A similar pit was identified on
the surface in Q20.
(3) Wall 300: the main wall, built over the top of Pit 94/44, and here up to
1.50 m. in width.
(4) Wall 304: a mud-brick wall also aligned roughly north-south, possibly
contemporary with (3).
(5) a room with narrow mud-brick walls occupying the south half of Q19.
(6) a stone-lined drain or water-conduit, which cuts through the mud-brick
of phase 4, and could be contemporary with Wall 303.
(7) Wall 303: a much narrower stone wall with some mud-brick footings,
which runs north-south at a distance of about 50 cm. parallel to the
inside face of Wall 300, but in places above it.
(8) Wall 301/302: two conjoined, narrow stone built walls to the west of
Wall 303, but of a later architectural phase.
(9) To the west of Walls 301/302/303, a large pit (94/41) containing much
Hellenistic (or perhaps slightly earlier) pottery (see below), and cut
through a surface associated with Walls 301/302.
While the relative dating of Wall 300 and the phase 5 room remains uncer-
tain, it is clear that phases 6 to 8 are later; probably all of phases 2 to 8 are of
Iron Age date, but we do not yet have enough stratified material associated with
the architecture, or a close enough knowledge of the Iron Age ceramic sequence,
to say more than this. However, the pit representing phase 9 is almost certainly
of Hellenistic date, and since it is later than Wall 303 which post-dates Wall 300,
it seems likely that the main fortification wall is of pre-Classical Iron Age date,
and had been built over by Hellenistic times.
The Northern end (Fig. 5; Pls XXII (b)-XXIV (b))
In order to investigate the depth of deposits and their date, an east-west
strip of four 10 x 10m. squares was opened at the north-west end of the
mound, in H-K20. This allowed us to broach a sequence of superimposed
layers, from late foundations at the summit of the mound at the east end of
K20 (at approx. +100.5 m.) to the lowest floor level in H20 some 6-5 m. lower
down (at +94 m.). Since at each level we are only nibbling at the frayed edges of
the architecture it would be premature to attempt a coherent description of the
buildings, and what follows is a very summary account concentrating on the
salient points of interest. Moreover, because in two places the relationship
between the main architectural levels remains unclear, we are not yet able to
assign a definitive numbering sequence; this will have await next season.
Starting from the east end, in K20 the first building level beneath the
plough soil was represented by a stone foundation running northeast to south-
west over a length of more than 11 m. It stood in a shallow trench packed
around with loose dark soil, in a technique also observed by us in L20 to the
east. In the absence of associated stratified deposits, we cannot suggest a date
for this architecture: it could be Byzantine or earlier.
The next phase or phases in K20 are present only as intrusive pits. Some of
these were no more than shallow circular cuts, but at least two in K20 and a
third in J20 were carefully formed bell-shaped pits. They had been dug from a
level now eroded from the surface, so that their upper rim nowhere survived
intact; their sides were coated in thick hard yellowish plaster, and so were their
bases, although in each case there was a distinct layer of black ash at the base of

could be as much as 2-80 m. (Pit 94/07 in J20c).34 In J19 to the south there is a
large modern robber pit, and in its western side one could observe a fourth such
pit in section: its profile was preserved closer to its mouth, showing a total
depth of 1.18 m., and a diameter at the top of 0.48 m. or less. Curiously there
was the skeleton of a young sheep (or goat) lying at the base of this pit, over the
ashy floor, but this can hardly reflect its normal function. We think it most likely
that these pits were used for grain storage, with the episode of burning designed
either to harden the clay linings, or to cleanse the pit before reuse. The date of
these pits remains problematic: that in J19 was truncated one metre below the
mound surface, and cannot therefore be placed very late, and Pit 94/07 yielded a
considerable quantity of Iron Age pottery. On the other hand, others in I-K20
are cut through architecture close to the surface, and their contents included
Byzantine material. They could have stood open for long periods on a deserted
site, and on balance, the easiest solution would be to attribute them provision-
ally to the Hellenistic period.
The next architectural remains belong to a building of mud-brick walls on
stone foundations (Plate XXIII (a)). In the north half of K20 at this phase there
was a triangular space, which from its shape and the number of ovens must
have been open to the sky. There was a row of three small clay-walled ovens,
ranged along the south side of a wall running north-east to south-west; at the
east end was another fire installation, a larger circular oven with hard clay floor
over a bed of round river pebbles; and a fifth smaller one between this and the
row of three. Beyond the cross wall to the east there were still further hearths,
but all were very close to the modern surface and eroded almost to their base.
At its west end the north wall meets a second wall at a sharp angle; this second
wall is effectively the south side of the courtyard, and at the same time the
north wall of a room whose floor lay as much as 65 cm. lower than that of the
courtyard, but was buried under a deep mass of destruction debris similar to
that in the open yard.
Further to the west beyond another wall in the south-east quarter of J20
another fire installation of the same phase is represented by an area of hard
smooth clay, sloping sharply down towards the north and burnt black. It is not
clear if this is an open space or a roofed area, and to make sense of the archi-
tecture it will be necessary to extend our excavation southwards. At present, it
seems likely that these are the informal domestic parts of a substantial building,
to which stone slab foundations uncovered in the north half of J20 belonged,
and whose northern continuation has been removed by the erosion of the
mound. Walls of clean yellow mud-brick were standing on these slabs to a
height of 80 cm., and it seems certain that a sequence of floors culminating with
the burnt courtyard level, observed in the side of Pit 94/13 in K20a/c, belonged
with these walls. We were at first inclined to assign this building and the court-
yard to the Early Iron Age, but there is not enough securely stratified pottery to
make this certain, and we cannot rule out an earlier date. This would not be
contradicted by the discovery within its confines of the stone seal with hiero-
glyphic inscription described below (?8).
Moving down to 120 takes us into earlier levels. A wall of mud-brick on
stone foundations runs across the middle of the square from north-east to

south-west. The erosion of the mound has removed most of the associated
deposits to its north-west, although the foundations of a cross-wall are present,
but to the south-east there is a rough surface, sloping slightly down to the south,
which must represent a courtyard floor (Plate XXIII (b)). On this floor was a
deep deposit of burned debris, including burnt mud-bricks, some of which had
clearly toppled southwards from off the stone foundations of the wall. The
eastern and southern limits of this court were not reached, but to the west there
are two cross walls with stone foundations side by side, that to the east having
been surmounted with mud-brick; it is as yet uncertain whether these are in part
contemporary or belong to different phases. Within the burnt debris, and in
the subsequent cleaner clayey filling of the courtyard, there was a good quantity
of sherds some of which come from well known Late Bronze Age types, includ-
ing red and "orange" burnished ware, libation arms and lentoid flasks (see ?7
below).
The building level just described extends through the west side of 120 into
the extreme south-east corner of H20, where two successive and very similar
phases of stone-built walls were excavated ("phases a and b"), one or both pre-
sumably contemporary with the 120 courtyard. Below these was a thick layer of
clay packing for phase b, sealing a very uneven deposit of loose black ash and
burnt brick and clay ("phase c"). This represented the destruction of an earlier
level, on the floor of which several pottery vessels had been standing, at
+95.20 m. In the very base of the phase b clay packing, but not, as far as we
could tell, in the destruction level proper, was a scatter of 7 similar crescent-
shaped objects, one of them of fired, the others of unbaked clay (KLT 8-14, 36;
see ?8 below). Whatever their purpose, these "crescents" are a firm chronological
indicator of the Middle Bronze Age, and imply that phase c belongs to that
period or earlier. There is little to be said of the architecture of phase c, because
there is an absence of walls and we are probably in an open courtyard. At the
extreme east side of H20 there is perhaps the remnant of a northwest-southeast
wall running under unexcavated levels of 120. Some 3 m. to its west there was
a post-hole, and further west again there are two fire installations, one larger
and shallow, the other smaller and deep. Lying on the surface of the large
hearth (FI 94/6) there was a good deal of burnt bone, including at least five
completely blackened horn-cores from a cervid of some kind.
Beneath phase c there is another layer of clay packing, which in its turn lies
over a thick layer of destruction debris, of loose black ash and fragments of
brick (phase d). Much of this deposit had been removed by a large robber hole
from earlier years, but what remains forms the south-east side of a room 5.25 m.
long, its floor at +94-72 m. (Plate XXIV (a)), more than 6 m. below the top of
the mound. The building had clay-plastered mud-brick walls standing up to
85 cm. high and benches against the south and east walls, on which stood two
pottery vessels and at least three unbaked clay vessels. On the other side of the
south-east wall was the extreme north-east corner of a second room, in which
there was an equal depth of burnt destruction deposit, covering as many as five
badly distorted vessels which had probably been of unbaked clay before the
conflagration (Plate XXIV (b)). This deposit continues under the southern baulk
into square J19, and had to be left for excavation in a future season. The
extremely coarse nature of these domestic vessels makes them difficult to place
chronologically, but one would expect them to belong to the early Middle
Bronze or latest Early Bronze period.
In summary, the 40 x 10 m. strip across the north end of the tepe has pro-
vided definite evidence of building levels from the Middle and Late Bronze
156

KILISE TEPE 1994
Ages. Below phase d in H20 it is evident that there are perhaps 3 m. or more of
archaeological deposit before the natural conglomerate is reached, and we there-
fore expect there to be Early Bronze and Chalcolithic levels as well; indeed
sherds of this date were collected from the surface on earlier surveys. Towards
the summit in K20 it remains to be established whether the highest stratified
sequence comes down into the Iron Age, to which the pottery in the pits cer-
tainly belongs. The wall foundations above this evidently come after a total
break in occupation, and do not relate to the alignment of earlier levels, but the
clearance of surface soil in a 10 m. strip joining Q20 to K20 revealed the foun-
dations of more than one building probably contemporary with them. They pre-
sumably belong to the Hellenistic or Byzantine era, both of which are attested
elsewhere on the site.
6. POTTERY FROM THE SURFACE COLLECTION
(D. Collon)
All the surface pottery was kept and washed. Diagnostic sherds were then
selected and these were recorded and, where relevant, their distribution was
mapped. The selection from which drawings were made forms the basis for the
present discussion.
The surface pottery is naturally most plentiful for the later periods which lie
nearest the surface. However, there was very little material that was recognisably
post-Byzantine. Fragments of trefoil lips were recorded exclusively on the west-
ern and southern slopes but their chronological range is so vast that they cannot
be used as a dating criterion where they are all that remain of a vessel. A distin-
guishing feature of the Kilise Tepe pottery at all periods is its dependence on
grit as opposed to vegetable temper. Except in the case of pithoi (Fig. 13), all
vessels are wheel-made unless otherwise specified.
Byzantine (Fig. 6:1-11)
The site of Alahan, off the same main road less than 50 km. north of Kilise
Tepe, provides the best published corpus for the area (Williams 1985). Although
the distinctive "Spiral Burnished Ware" recorded at Alahan was not identified
on the surface at Kilise Tepe, there are numerous sherds of Monastic Ware; this
has also been recorded at Dag Pazar near Alahan. There are close parallels for
the designs, with swirls, circles, dots and possibly more representational motifs
painted with great freedom in red-brown to brown paint on a buff to orange-red
(apricot) ground and, as at Alahan, there was no clear indication of a slip or
wash. However, whereas the Alahan wares are described as being micaceous, the
Kilise Tepe examples are not, and, although they are grit-tempered, they do not
have the "distinctly gritty texture" and "medium to large-sized white lime
particles and small dark grits" of the Alahan Monastic Ware. It seems that the
Kilise Tepe Monastic Ware was either made locally or derived from another site.
The sherds from Kilise Tepe all seem to have belonged to closed shapes which
were also the most popular at Alahan; there was no example of the distinctive
rim decoration of the Alahan Monastic Ware bowls which were, in any case,
rare. The ware, like the monastery at Alahan, probably dates to the 5th and
6th centuries.
Most of the Monastic Ware sherds were found in a diagonal band across
the site from squares J18 and K20-18 in the north-west to Rll-10 in the south-
east; there were also some sherds in the south-western part of the site and single
scattered examples elsewhere. The highest concentration in any one square was
four sherds in square L13 and five in square 013.

Late Roman and Byzantine plain and cooking wares (Fig. 6:12-15)
Sherds of these wares are often difficult to differentiate from cooking wares
of other periods because they are of the same gritty and brittle fabric. Wares of
this type with fine horizontal grooving, undulating strap handles and jugs with
handles with a central groove and a thumb impression or knob at the top, which
is level with the top of the rim, have been considered to fall within this category.
They are scattered all over the mound with a concentration in the south-east.
However, there is a noticeable gap in squares where the complex of Byzantine
buildings may have been located and this, together with the fact that there are
no close parallels either with the cooking wares from Alahan, or with the mid-
7th century Byzantine pottery from the well at Anemurium (Williams 1985; id.
1977), casts doubt upon the validity of this attribution.

Roman fine wares (Fig. 6:16-18)

certainly the virtual absence of these easily identifiable wares at Kilise Tepe

Hellenistic wares (Figs. 7-8)
These distinctive wares were found all over the site with the exception of
the north-west corner. The heaviest concentrations were in the north-east. They
seem predominantly to belong to the 3rd to 2nd centuries B.C. There are two
main types, both generally with no visible temper: "A" (specified in the cata-
logue) is harder-fired than "B" and makes a clink when struck. In very few cases
was a good coating of gloss preserved.

a. Bowls with inturned rim (Fig. 7:1-3)
Numerous sherds were found of this, the most common shape, generally in
a beige or greyish-brown fabric with a greyish-brown or blackish-brown slip on
the interior and unevenly on the exterior, as if the slip had been poured into the
vessel, swilled around and poured out as the pot was turned and allowed to run
down the exterior towards the base; it is quite dark where it is thickest. The
excavations produced some complete profiles which are illustrated in Fig. 7.
These bowls are dated to the 3rd and early 2nd centuries B.C. (Crowfoot et al.
1957, p. 224 Fig. 38 from Samaria; Waage 1948, P1. III Shapes 70-9 from
Antioch; Hayes 1991, Figs XIV and LVII from Paphos).

Iron Age (Figs. 9-10)
There is another possible gap in the sequence between the Middle Iron Age
and the Hellenistic period but this may be because the wares between c. 600 and
300 B.c. have not been recognised. There are several parallels from Tarsus for
the remainder of the Iron Age, and the subdivisions suggested by Goldman
(1963, p. 20) have been adopted. Due to their diversity, surface distribution of
these wares at Kilise Tepe was not plotted.
Late Iron Age (Fig. 9:1-2)
At Tarsus this period has been divided into a 6th century phase and a
7th century Assyrian phase. The sample from Kilise Tepe is too small for such
a division to be relevant here.

(Goldman 1963, Fig. 92 No. 1298).

Conclusion
With the exception of one probable gap between c. 100 B.C. and the 4th cen-
tury A.D., and one possible gap between c. 600 and 300 B.C., occupation at Kilise
Tepe seems to have been continuous from the Early Bronze Age until the 6th or
7th century A.D. The scant evidence for the Middle Bronze Age in the surface
survey does not seem to be reflected in the excavations, where this period seems
to be well represented. Early Bronze Age levels have also been reached in the
excavations but the depth of deposits beneath these levels would indicate the
possibility of Chalcolithic occupation although no pottery of this period was
recognised in the surface survey.
7. EXCAVATED MATERIAL: PRE-CLASSICAL POTTERY (D. Symington)
The pottery repertoire encountered during our first season at Kilise Tepe
can be described as a pleasing assemblage in which a large percentage of pottery
types received some form of surface treatment and painted wares were not
uncommon. As already observed by Mellaart (1955, 1958) and D. French
(1965), through their respective survey work in the Goksu valley, the ceramic
affinities of Kilise Tepe, then known as Maltepe, lay mainly with Cilicia to the
south. Future investigations should define this trend more clearly, but our first
impressions would largely confirm their findings. Pottery parallels can frequently
be drawn with the sequence of Tarsus and Mersin. The fact that Cilicia itself
was subject to alternating influences over the millennia coming from central
Anatolia and North Syria respectively, would also have affected the Goksu
valley, since Cilicia's contact with the plateau was not only maintained through
the Cilician Gates, but equally via the valley of the G6ksu. Cultural relations
with the Konya plain to the north, on the other hand, cannot be fully evaluated
at present since we still lack a complete ceramic sequence for that region which
would allow a comparative analysis for the Kilise Tepe material.
Much of the pottery corpus retrieved in 1994 awaits more detailed study
and the following represents an outline of the main pottery groups as we
encountered them in the course of the excavation.
Iron Age (Figs. 14-15)
Besides the surface finds, the Iron Age pottery recovered originated mainly
from a series of pits in K20 and J20, as well as mixed deposits in the latter
172

KILISE TEPE 1994
square. It was also found in some quantitites in R18, although not in association
with any architectural features.
The observation made at other sites, that there was no clear ceramic break
between the Late Bronze Age and the Iron Age in the production of household
wares, was also found to be the case at Kilise Tepe. The shallow bowls and
plates recorded in large quantities among Iron Age pottery would fit equally well
into a Late Bronze Age context. They occurred in two distinct wares. (1) The
plain type, resembling most closely Hittite "drab" ware made in buff, grit and
organic tempered fabrics with smoothed surfaces. (2) A slipped and burnished
type which is almost as common as the plain. Vessels are covered with a
slip/wash which is frequently dictated by the colour of the fabric, varying from
red to brown to beige. Both surfaces are horizontally burnished to a low lustre.
The plain and monochrome bowls and plates occur primarily with slightly thick-
ened inverted rims and since they were repeatedly found side by side we can be
certain of their contemporary use.
A quite considerable percentage of the Iron Age pottery recovered at Kilise
Tepe belonged to the category of painted wares. As already established by pre-
vious survey work (Mellaart 1955, 115, 117), the Kilise Tepe material (as that of
the G6ksu valley as a whole) belongs to the painted pottery province of Cilicia
for which the Tarsus sequence represents our main source (Hanfmann in
Goldman 1963). Comparable material is also reported from Mersin (Barnett
1939), Karatepe (Darga 1986) and very recently from Kinet Hoyiik (Gates 1994,
261 Fig. 7) in eastern Cilicia. The site of Porsuk situated on the northern
approach to the Cilician Gates provides an additional source of comparable
material. Here Cilician type pottery was found in the Early Iron Age, level IV
(Dupre 1983).
By far the most common form of decoration encountered at Kilise Tepe are
red bands on a light background which decorated much of the household pot-
tery and, it would appear over a long period of time. Bands are painted, often
carelessly, on mainly closed-shaped vessels made of medium or coarse fabrics
which are invariably covered with a cream wash or slip. They are normally
applied horizontally in colours varying from orange-red to reddish-brown to
pink. Equally variable is the thickness of the paint which ranges from a thick
glossy to a fugitive appearance (Fig. 14:1). This rather large pottery group which
requires further study, nevertheless appears to have sound parallels with the red-
banded ware discussed in the Tarsus report. Here it is said to have occurred
throughout the Iron Age (Goldman 1963, 34, 58 f.), and indeed still decorates
vessels in the Hellenistic period (Tarsus I, Fig. 134:220). At the other end of the
time scale, red-banded pottery was also noted for the final LBA phase (LB IIb)
at Tarsus (Goldman 1956, Fig. 337:1362-63; cf. Masat level I, Ozgii 1982, 102
Fig. 34), and at Kilise Tepe it was found in small quantities in the LBA destruc-
tion layer in 120 (see below).
The remaining painted pottery can be roughly grouped into three cate-
gories: (1) Brown/red-painted on a white/cream slip or wash; (2) red-painted
on an orange background; (3) bichrome ware.
(1) The most favoured painted pottery type is the first group which far out-
numbered the sherds belonging to the second category. It appears to correspond
to Cilician buff-painted and Cilician white-painted from Tarsus (Goldman 1963,
48 ff.), although this distinction cannot be made with any confidence for the
Kilise Tepe material at present. This painted ware was also collected from some
forty sites on the Cilician survey, referred to by Seton-Williams as "white
painted" (1954, 137 f.). The pattern repertoire is, as one would expect, closely
173

ANATOLIAN STUDIES
related to Cilician pottery (Goldman 1963, 45 ff.) and includes the following
designs:
(a) Compass-drawn circles or targets appear on a variety of sherds from
fine to medium coarse vessels, belonging to bowls (kraters?) and closed
shapes, executed in brown and blackish-brown paint of varying thick-
ness (Fig.10:7-9). At Tarsus, vessels with concentric circle decoration
appear already in the Early Iron Age and in Greece they mark the
beginning of the Proto-Geometric style (Goldman 1963, 46, Fig. 56:28,
33). A variant are pendant semi-concentric circles bordering on horizon-
tal bands as seen on Fig. 14:2-3, forming part of a metopal arrangement
with hatched decoration. The comparable examples of semi-targets
at Porsuk (Dupre 1983, 68, PI. 50:42) and at Tarsus (Goldman 1963,
Fig. 55:16) are both dated to the Early Iron Age. A fairly certain
candidate of the Middle Iron Age is the fragment with intersecting
bands enclosing fine lines and concentric circles (Fig. 14:4), a well
known type of decoration on globular trefoil jugs at Tarsus (Goldman
1963, (Fig. 64:334), Fig. 68:445-6) and Karatepe (Darga 1986, 398,
P1. V. ).
(b) Linear decoration with bands and fine lines painted in black or brown
on the exterior or interior of open vessels. Also represented are frag-
ments of closed shapes (Fig. 10:4; Fig. 14:5-9). Some of the sherds with
linear design are extremely fine. Most of this material is probably
Middle Iron Age (cf. Goldman 1963, Fig. 67:407, Fig. 69:473,
Fig. 115:67A).
(c) The hatched lozenge is represented by a single sherd (Fig. 14:10), com-
bined here with a single wavy line and multiple horizontal lines, not a
common combination, but a similarly decorated jug is known from
Mersin (Barnett 1939, 105, PI. 46:7).
(d) Wide hatched bands on the exterior of cups or bowls covering the rim
and part of the shoulder have a perfect match in the Early Iron Age
level at Porsuk (Dupre 1983, 67 P1. 51:51, 46).
(e) Handle decoration consists of simple cross-strokes (Fig. 15:1) or com-
bined with longitudinal bands (Fig. 10:6; Fig. 15:2-3), widely known
from other sites (Goldman 1963, Fig. 64:321, Fig. 66:378).
(2) The second painted pottery group, which made up a much smaller cor-
pus of sherds, is red painted on a plain orange fabric (grit tempered medium to
coarse). It was also recorded at Porsuk in level IV where it constitutes the most
common painted ware (Dupre 1983, 58 f.). The designs include lattice patterns
(Fig. 15:4-5), pendant triangles, criss-cross patterns on ledge rims and diagonal
strokes on jar rims.
(3) Bichrome ware. Only a handful of bichrome sherds came to light. Most
were decorated with red and black paint on a cream background with simple
linear designs. Exceptions are the two illustrated pieces both of which were sur-
face finds (Fig. 10:8, 12).

Mycenaean vessel (Fig. 15:6)
Seven sherds of a Mycenaean vessel, which could be partly restored, came
from a pit (94/23) in K20 that contained a large percentage of Iron Age pottery.
It is made of very fine light orange clay, with no visible temper, and decorated
with a repeated series of fine lines flanked by broad bands executed in orange-
red paint on an apricot slip. The upper part of the shoulder is missing, but
traces of a handle attachment are still visible. Our vessel (in as far as it is pre-
served) comes closest to the category of squat stirrup-jars of LHIIIA/B as dis-
cussed and illustrated by Mountjoy (1986, 79 f. FS178, Fig. 94:1-2 LHIIIA2,
p. 106 f. FS180, Fig. 130:1 LHIIIB1), dated to c. 1360-1220 B.C. (Table 1 on p. 8).
Mycenaean imports into Anatolia35 (leaving aside west Anatolian sites) are
reported from Fraktin (LHIIIC stirrup jar, unpublished) and more recently
from Ma?at where several Mycenaean vessels of the LHIIIB type were found
(T. Ozgtui 1978, 127 f., PI. 83:la-b, P1. 84:1; idem 1982, 102 f., P1. 47:5,6).
Considerable amounts of Mycenaean LHIIIC pottery (most of which is
locally made) were found together with Hittite plain wares at Tarsus in LBIIb,
after the destruction of the Hittite LBIIa levels (Goldman 1956, 205 ff.). It was
also collected from a number of coastal sites in Cilicia (Mee 1978, 150 and
passim).
A re-assessment of the Tarsus LHIIIC pottery by E. French concluded that
its affinities lay with the material from Cyprus (1975, 73 f.). The evidence for
the G6ksu valley regarding LHIIIC pottery is as yet inconclusive, although
D. French on his survey remarked on the absence of Mycenaean wares (1965,
185).

Among the domestic pottery, plain wares occurred in buff or reddish-brown
fabrics with plates and shallow bowls as the most prominent shape. Plates with
simple or broad rim profiles (Fig. 16:1-4), not encountered in Iron Age con-
texts, were found in 120 and on an occupational surface in J20 (1324). They are
characteristic of the Late Hittite period at Bogazkby (Fischer 1963, 66, Taf.
100:910, Taf. 101:916, 921) and at Tarsus LBII (Goldman 1956, Fig. 384:1134,
1137). Other shapes with a plain finish are small lentoid flasks (Fig. 16:5) and
ring-base vases (Fig. 16:7), the latter not a common shape, but one which has
counterparts at Ma?at level II (Ozgiiq 1978, 124, P1. 48:2) and also at Bogazkoy
(Fischer 1963, 148, Taf. 123:1075). Slipped and burnished kitchen wares were
equally in evidence (Fig. 16:7-9), including a jar with the slip carried over the
rim forming a band in the interior, a technique more commonly known from
the early part of the Late Bronze Age.
Among the finer wares we recorded the handle of a white slipped lentoid
flask in the destruction debris of 120 (Fig. 16:10; cf. Fischer 1963, 128 f., Taf.
47:473; Dupre 1983, 49 nos. 147-149, P1. 42). White slipped pottery, relatively
common at Bogazk6y, was sparsely represented at Kilise Tepe amounting to a
small number of mainly unstratified body sherds and a cylindrical-shaped frag-
ment from the surface collection in R10 (Fig. 11:10).

A rather striking pottery type, which stood out amongst the ceramics at
Kilise Tepe, was a very fine dense red burnished ware of exceptional quality and
technique. It first came to our attention through surface finds scattered over the
east side of the mound in the form of neck and handle fragments belonging to a
lentoid flask (Fig. 11:1), and was later found in the above-mentioned Late
Bronze Age destruction level in 120, as well as in J20. We recorded a consider-
able amount of this ware consisting of various handles and fragments of bowls,
flasks and arm-shaped vessels, all made in the characteristic fabric and
technique: a very fine hard homogeneous orange-red or reddish brown fabric,
fired at high temperature to an even colour with carefully burnished surfaces.

ANATOLIAN STUDIES
Because of its great density the pottery feels particularly heavy.
This material is either identical, or very similar, to a ware widely distributed
in the Eastern Mediterranean which is referred to by some authors as "red
lustrous ware", and is associated with a number of characteristic shapes: the
spindle bottle, the arm-shaped vessel and the lentoid or pilgrim flask (amongst
others). This ware has also the distinction that wherever it was encountered-
Cyprus, Palestine, North Syria, Anatolia-it was regarded as imported on
account of its outstanding technology (and shapes) which could not be matched
within the respective ceramic corpuses. North Syria has generally been favoured
as the place of origin, but Cyprus and "upper Cilicia" were also suggested (J.-C.
Courtois 1979, 90). The considerable literature devoted to this pottery, is sum-
marized in a recent and comprehensive study on the subject which concluded
that the centre of production was in fact Cyprus. The chief reasons for the argu-
ment are that more than 50% of the "red lustrous wheel-made ware" was found
in Cyprus and that the pottery marks, many of which are of Cypro-Minoan
character, were incised before firing (Eriksson 1993, 149 ff.).
The lentoid flask fragment (assembled with pieces from Q18, R11, Q14,
Fig. 11:1) with its long conically shaped neck and collar has a close parallel with
a specimen from Porsuk (Dupre 1983, 49 no. 144, P1. 24), but equally with
examples from Bogazkoy (Fischer 1963, Taf. 49:488, Taf. 50:496) and in Old
Kingdom context at Inandik (T. Ozgiiu 1988, 80, Fig. 17, 18 a-b). However,
none of the above comparable examples are made in red lustrous ware. Lentoid
flasks have a long history in Anatolia36 and occur in a number of variants
(Fischer 1963, 50 ff.). They are particularly widespread at central Anatolian
sites. One would expect the Kilise Tepe vessel, which is of exceptional quality
and of fair size (original height c. 50 cm.), to have served as a cult vessel,37 as
illustrated on the Bitik vase which depicts in one of the registers a worshipper
with a lentoid flask strapped to his back (T. Ozgiiu 1988, P1. 67:1).
An unusual version of the LBA lentoid flask is the type with a fenestrated
stand which has been associated with red lustrous wheel-made ware (Eriksson
1993, 27 Fig. 6). At Kilise Tepe a fragment with most of the fenestrated part
preserved was found in an exploratory section in R18, and a piece with a similar
pattern was collected by Mellaart on our mound, then referred to as Maltepe
(1958, 330 n. 62, P1. IV:36). This piece is described as coarse, and our excavated
Kilise Tepe example is not made in red lustrous ware, but has buff fabric with a
grey core and traces of a red wash on the exterior. Its incised decoration is a
herring-bone pattern interrupted by vertical grooves.38 It is not pierced.
Libation arms (Fig. 17)
The most striking shape in the repertoire is the arm-shaped vessel, also
referred to as libation arm: a long tube-like vessel closed at one end, and at the
other terminating in a hand holding a small cup. While the main body is wheel-
made the hand and cup are modelled by hand (Eriksson 1993, 17). Libation
arms have a wide distribution in Anatolia in Late Bronze Age contexts
(Eriksson 1993, 27, Cat. pp. 257-275), particularly at Bogazkoy-Hattusa where

In addition the bottom part of another arm-shaped vessel came from a

found at Kilise Tepe have pronounced twisted wheel-turning marks on the
inside.

The cult function of the arm-shaped object as a libation vessel has been
discussed on a number of occasions and attempts were made to identify it in
Hittite texts among the ceremonial vessels associated with the act of libating
(sipant- ).40
Dense red burnished wares were also picked up on neighbouring sites in the
Goksu valley at (ingantepe,41 Orentepe,4 Kozlubulak43 and on mounds in the
coastal region near Silifke at Tekirkoy44 and further east at Tomiikkale,45 Tirmil
and Cavu?lu (see n. 46) in Rough Cilicia. The evidence for "red lustrous" wares
in the Cilician plain, however, is surprisingly scanty. This observation was
already made by Gjerstad who remarked on its rare occurrence during his
Cilician survey which covered the area from Anamur in the west to Misis in the
centre of the plain.46 It was barely represented at Tarsus or Mersin (a fact also
noted by Eriksson 1993, 132 f.), although Late Bronze Age levels were explored
extensively at both sites. The evidence so far appears to attribute a special role
to the Goksu valley regarding dense red burnished ware and equally focuses on
the maritime importance of the coastal region around Silifke, whether one
accepts the thesis of a single production centre of this ware in Cyprus, or not.
Middle Bronze Age (Figs. 18; 19:1)
The ceramic sequence continued in the adjoining square H20 to the west
with pottery of the Middle Bronze Age. Our evidence for this period consists of
a series of almost whole or partly preserved vessels found in situ in a burnt
occupational level of phase c, and a representative selection of MBA sherds
from the same phase. Although the pots belong to the category of simple
kitchen ware, they are on the whole carefully made with evidence of slips
applied to the exterior or both sides and in some cases low lustre burnishing.
The jugs have ovoid bodies with slightly rounded bases, indicating the use of pot
stands or their setting into the floor. None of the upper neck and rim parts have
survived to determine their final shape (Fig. 18:1). Of a more coarse fabric and
hand made are the cooking pots (Fig. 18:2, 4) and a crudely made lid with flared
sides and a broken-off basket handle on the top (Fig. 18:3). The latter can be
paralleled by two rather smaller examples from Tarsus (one decorated with a
combed pattern) found in MBA contexts (Goldman 1956, Fig. 372:921-2).
A typical feature of our MBA pot handles is a longitudinal shallow groove
on the top (Fig. 18:5), which was also observed on vessels from Karahiiyiik of
the same period displayed in the Konya Museum. It also occurs on MBA teapot
